title: Foundations of Data Science
text: Welcome

title: Foundations of Data Science
text:
About This Book
This is a book about exploratory data analysis in Python. Exploratory data analysis, or EDA, is the approach described in John Tukey’s classic text of the same name: examining data directly through visualizations and broad summary statistics in order to generate hypotheses and understand general trends, prior to and often in lieu of formal statistical modeling. As the volume and complexity of available data have grown, the need for careful application of these techniques has only increased.
Our aim is to teach a small set of composable tools thoroughly rather than a large catalog of methods superficially. Nearly everything in the book is built from a handful of operations—selecting and filtering rows, grouping and aggregating, joining tables, reshaping between long and wide formats, and mapping columns to the visual properties of a plot. Once these operations are understood, they apply without modification to data that arrives as a spreadsheet, a map, a corpus of documents, a network of citations, or a collection of photographs. The second half of the book is essentially a demonstration of that claim.
The book is written with the humanities and humanistic social sciences particularly in mind. Our examples are drawn from demographic indicators, census data, encyclopedia articles, citation structures, and image collections, and the questions we ask of them are the kinds of questions that arise in humanistic inquiry. No prior programming experience is assumed.

title: Foundations of Data Science
text:
Software
All of the code in the book is written in Python. We use three libraries as our foundation:

Polars for representing and manipulating tabular data, using its expression system and method-chaining style.
plotnine for visualization, which implements Leland Wilkinson’s grammar of graphics in the tradition of R’s ggplot2.
Quarto notebooks (.qmd files) for mixing code, output, and prose in a single document.

Additional libraries appear as the material requires them: GeoPandas for spatial geometry, requests for calling web APIs, spaCy for natural language processing, NetworkX for graphs, and OpenCV together with pre-trained neural networks for images. In each case we wrap the library behind a thin set of helper functions in a local funs.py module so that the analysis stays in the tabular, expression-based idiom established in Part I. The code behind each helper is shown before it is used; nothing is meant to be magic.
Readers new to programming can run everything through Google Colab with no setup. Readers comfortable with the command line may prefer uv for managing a local Python installation. Every dataset used in the book is in the public domain and is distributed alongside the text.

title: Foundations of Data Science
text:
Part I: Core
The first ten chapters develop the core grammar of data manipulation and visualization. They use a common running example—a dataset of 135 countries with demographic, economic, and well-being indicators—so that attention stays on the techniques rather than on learning a new dataset each week.
Chapter 1, Introduction, situates EDA within the history of statistical computing, from the S language at Bell Labs through R and into the Python data science ecosystem. It covers running Python code, variables, functions, importing modules, reading a CSV file into a DataFrame, and the code formatting conventions used throughout the text.
Chapter 2, Data Types and Expressions, explains why every column carries a type, what goes wrong when the type is incorrect (a ZIP code silently converted to an integer loses its leading zero; a single "n/a" turns an entire numeric column into text), and how to cast between types. It then introduces Polars expressions, the deferred descriptions of computation that underlie every operation in the rest of the book.
Chapter 3, Organizing Data I: Rows and Columns, covers the verbs that act on a single table: sorting rows, selecting columns, filtering rows, renaming, removing duplicates, and creating or modifying columns. It also introduces the pipe-and-chain style of writing analyses as a sequence of steps.
Chapter 4, Visualizing Data I: The Grammar of Graphics, introduces plotnine. Rather than a menu of chart types, the grammar treats a plot as a mapping from columns to aesthetics—position, color, size, label—rendered by a choice of geometry. The chapter covers points, text, lines, and bars, along with layering multiple datasets in a single figure.
Chapter 5, Grouping and Aggregation, introduces group_by and agg, the operations that collapse many rows into one summary row per group. It develops aggregation as a form of row reduction and covers counting, grouping by multiple columns, and aggregating expressions rather than bare columns.
Chapter 6, Window Functions, addresses the complementary problem: computations that need group-level context but must return a value for every original row. It covers the over method, ordered windows and shifts, ranks and cumulative functions, rolling windows, and the choice between agg and over.
Chapter 7, Visualizing Data II: Statistics, Scales, and Polish, returns to graphics with the statistical layer—histograms, density plots, boxplots, and fitted models—and then covers scales, facets, labels, and themes. It closes with a set of best practices for producing figures that communicate clearly.
Chapter 8, Combining Tables, covers joins. It establishes primary and foreign keys, works through the family of equi-joins (inner, left, right, full, semi, anti, and cross), introduces filtering joins and conditional joins, and devotes a section to diagnosing joins that silently go wrong.
Chapter 9, Restructuring Data, develops the concept of the unit of observation and the definition of tidy data. It covers pivoting and unpivoting between wide and long formats, reshaping specifically for plotting, expanding list columns, and the distinction between restructuring that is reversible and aggregation that is not.
Chapter 10, Collecting Data, is about the practice rather than the theory: how to decide what tables you need, what variables and names they should have, how to store data in spreadsheets so it can be read cleanly, how to read imperfect files, and how to document decisions in a data dictionary. It engages directly with the questions of bias and power that arise whenever data is collected and organized.

title: Foundations of Data Science
text:
Part II: Applications
The remaining chapters apply the core grammar to data types that do not arrive as a tidy CSV. Each introduces a new kind of object—a geometry, an HTTP response, a token, a document vector, an edge list, a pixel array—and shows how to bring it into the tabular framework already established.
Chapter 11, Spatial Data, attaches a geometry to each observation. It covers points and polygons, coordinate reference systems and why projections matter, choropleth maps, interactive visualization, and the distinctively spatial operations: spatial joins, nearest-neighbor joins, buffers, dissolving boundaries, exploding multi-part geometries, and simplification. The examples use U.S. metropolitan statistical areas and census data.
Chapter 12, Data APIs and Corpus Construction, moves from files to servers. It teaches the mechanics of HTTP requests, status codes, pagination, caching, and flattening nested JSON—and then uses them to build something. Over the course of the chapter we assemble a corpus of Wikipedia articles on nineteenth-century British and Irish novelists, collecting metadata, full text, internal links, revision histories, page views, and cross-language links. It also covers what it means to be a polite client, how to define a population, and how to make a snapshot reproducible.
Chapter 13, Textual Data: Annotations, opens that corpus. Using spaCy, it turns unstructured prose into a table of annotated tokens with parts of speech, lemmas, named entities, and dependency relations. Once text is a table, the familiar operations apply: word frequencies, n-grams and collocations, keyword-in-context, and complexity metrics all become grouping and filtering problems.
Chapter 14, Textual Data: Documents as Vectors, shifts from words within documents to documents themselves. It develops TF-IDF weighting, reinterprets weighted term counts geometrically so that each document becomes a point in a high-dimensional space, and uses that geometry to measure distances between documents, project the corpus into two dimensions, and compare the English and French Wikipedia treatments of the same authors.
Chapter 15, Network Data, represents relationships as nodes and edges. Using the link structure of the same Wikipedia corpus, it covers building a network from an edge list, centrality measures, clustering, co-citation networks, and directed graphs. It then closes the loop with Part II’s other chapters by constructing distance and nearest-neighbor networks from the document vectors of Chapter 14.
Chapter 16, Image Data, treats images first as arrays of numbers—brightness, hue, saturation, and color distributions computed directly from pixels—and then as embeddings produced by large pre-trained neural networks. Because an embedding is just a vector, the geometric toolkit built for text carries over unchanged: images can be mapped with UMAP, compared by similarity, and classified into categories described in plain English without training a model.

title: Foundations of Data Science
text:
A Note on Structure
The chapters build on one another and are best read in order. Part I is cumulative by design, and Part II depends on it throughout. Within Part II, the Wikipedia corpus constructed in Chapter 12 is the shared substrate for Chapters 13, 14, and 15, and the vector representation developed for text in Chapter 14 is the same idea applied to images in Chapter 16. The connections are the point: the chapters are not a survey of unrelated methods but a demonstration that one set of tools reaches a surprisingly wide range of data.
Each chapter ends with a short Extensions section pointing to further reading, additional libraries, and methods that may be of interest for that specific kind of data.

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.1 Introduction
In this book, we focus on tools and techniques for exploratory data analysis, also known as EDA. Initially described in John Tukey’s classic text of the same name, EDA is a general approach to examining data through visualizations and broad summary statistics [1] [2]. It prioritizes studying data directly to generate hypotheses and ascertain general trends prior to and often in lieu of formal statistical modeling. The growth in both data volume and complexity has further increased the need for careful application of these exploratory techniques. In the intervening years, EDA techniques have become widely used within statistics, computer science, and many other data-driven fields and professions.
The histories of data programming and EDA are deeply entwined. Concurrent with Tukey’s development of exploratory data analysis (EDA), Rick Becker, John Chambers, and Allan Wilks of Bell Labs began developing software designed specifically for statistical computing. By 1980, the ‘S’ language was released for general distribution outside Bell Labs. It was followed by a popular series of books and updates, including ‘New S’ and ‘S-Plus’ [3] [4] [5] [6]. In the early 1990s, Ross Ihaka and Robert Gentleman produced a fully open-source implementation of S called ‘R’. The name ‘R’ was chosen both as a play on the previous letter in the alphabet and as a reference to the authors’ shared initial. Their implementation has become the de facto standard tool in statistics. More recently, many of R’s best ideas have been extended to other general-purpose programming languages such as Python and JavaScript.

title: 1  Introduction
text: Figure 1.1: Diagram of the process of exploratory data analysis

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.2 Data Science Ecosystem
The ideas and methods of exploratory data analysis (EDA) have been successfully adopted and extended in Python through a rich ecosystem of data science libraries. Python, originally created by Guido van Rossum in 1991, has evolved into one of the most popular programming languages for data analysis, machine learning, and scientific computing. Pandas, developed by Wes McKinney beginning in 2008, brought R-like data structures and manipulation capabilities to Python [7]. Matplotlib and, later, Seaborn provided comprehensive plotting capabilities, while plotnine implemented the grammar of graphics approach pioneered by ggplot2 in R. More recently, Polars has emerged as a high-performance alternative to pandas for large-scale data manipulation.
This Python ecosystem preserves the philosophy behind EDA: prioritizing interactive exploration, readable code, and the ability to move seamlessly between data manipulation, visualization, and analysis. We see this book as contributing to efforts to bring new communities into data analysis and to help shape that analysis by offering the humanities and humanistic social sciences powerful tools for data-driven inquiry. A visual summary of the steps of EDA is shown above in Fig. 1.1. We will see that the core chapters in this text map onto the steps outlined in the diagram.

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.3 Setup
While it is possible to read this book as a conceptual text, we expect that the majority of readers will eventually want to follow along with the code and examples given throughout the text. The first step is to obtain a working installation of Python with the necessary data science modules. For readers new to programming, we recommend getting started with Google Colab, which provides free access to a working Python session with no setup required. For users comfortable with the command line, the uv library is extremely powerful and reduces many of the pain points associated with other methods of setting up Python.
In addition to the Python software, following the examples in this text requires access to the datasets we use. Care has been taken to ensure that these are all in the public domain, making them easy to redistribute to readers. The materials and download instructions are available through the associated notebooks at the start of each chapter.
Learning to program is hard, and questions and issues will invariably arise in the process (even the most experienced users require help surprisingly frequently). As a first source of help, searching a question or error message in a search engine or a chat-based generative AI system will often pull up a solution directly related to your question. If you cannot find an immediate answer, the next best step is to find local, in-person help. While we’ve done our best in this static text to explain the concepts for working with Python, nothing beats talking to a real person.

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.4 Working with Notebooks
The supplemental materials include all the data and code needed to replicate the analyses and visualizations in the book. We include the exact code that is printed in the book. We have used Quarto notebooks (with an .qmd extension) to store this code, with a file corresponding to each chapter. Quarto notebooks are an excellent choice for data analysis because they allow us to mix code, visualizations, and explanations within the same file. In fact, the entire data science workflow—from initial exploration through final presentation—can be contained within a single notebook. Furthermore, Quarto notebooks can be executed from Google Colab, allowing you to run the notebooks via a one-click link.
The Quarto environment provides a convenient way to view and edit notebooks. A notebook interface is organized into cells that contain either code or formatted text (markdown). Running a code cell executes the Python code and displays the output directly below the cell, making the environment ideal for exploratory data analysis because we can experiment and immediately see the results. Code cells typically have a gray background and can be executed by clicking the run button or pressing Shift+Enter. When we read or create a dataset, we can inspect it by typing the variable name in a cell.

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.5 Running Python Code
Now let’s look at some examples of how to run Python code. In this book, we will show snippets of Python code and their output. Note that each snippet should be thought of as occurring in a code cell in a Quarto notebook. In one of its simplest forms, Python acts as a calculator. We can add 1 and 1 by typing 1 + 1 into a code cell; running the cell will display the output (2) below. In this book, we will present code and its output in a black box, with the Python code shown inside it and any output shown beneath. An example is given below.

1 + 1

2


In addition to returning a value, running Python code can also store values by creating new variables. Variables in Python are used to store anything—such as numbers, datasets, functions, or models—that we want to use again later. Each variable has a name we can use to access it in later code. To create a variable, we use the = (equals) symbol, with the name on the left and the expression that produces the value on the right. For example, we can create a new variable called mynum with the value 8 by running the following code.

mynum = 3 + 5

Notice that this code did not print any results because the result was saved to a new variable. We can now use our variable mynum in the same way we would use the number 8. For example, adding it to 1 yields the number nine:

mynum + 1

9


Variable names must start with a letter or an underscore, but can contain numbers after the first character. We recommend using only lowercase letters and underscores. This makes it easier to read the code later without having to remember whether and where you used capital letters.

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.6 Functions in Python
A function in Python takes a set of input values and returns an output value. Typically, a function has a format similar to the code below:

function_name(input1, input2)

Where input1 and input2 are the values we pass to the first and second arguments of the function. The number of arguments is not always two, of course. There may be any number of arguments, including zero. There may also be additional optional arguments with default values that can be modified. Let us look at an example function: round. This function returns a rounded version of a number. If you give the function a single number, it returns the nearest integer. For example, here is the rounded value of π:

round(3.14159)

3


The function has an additional optional parameter that specifies the number of significant digits. For example, this will round π to two significant digits:

round(3.14159, 2)

3.14


An alternative way to call the same function is to use named arguments, where the two input values are specified by name rather than by position:

round(number=3.14159, ndigits=2)

3.14


How do we know the inputs to each function and what they do? In this text, we will explain the names and usage of the required inputs for new functions as they are introduced. To learn more about all of the possible inputs to a function, we can consult the function’s documentation. Python has excellent built-in documentation that can be accessed using the help() function. Most Python modules also have extensive online documentation. For example, the Polars library has comprehensive documentation at https://pola.rs/. We will learn how to use numerous functions in the coming chapters, each of which will help us explore and understand data.

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.7 Data Science Modules
A major selling point of Python is its extensive collection of user-contributed modules, available through the Python Package Index (PyPI). Most of the core modules we will need are already installed in Google Colab, and all we need to do is import them. When extra modules are needed, we will include lines of code such as the following to install them from within the Colab environment.

! pip install requests --quiet

Once all modules are available, we’ll run code to load the modules we need into Python. Below is a common example of what we’ll have at the start of our scripts.

import polars as pl

from funs import *
from plotnine import *
from polars import col as c

The lines that start with import make a module available for use in Python. Any function available within the module can be accessed by writing the module name followed by a dot and the function name. The as clause creates a shortcut that makes the code easier to type. For example, after running the code above, we can call the read_csv function from Polars by typing pl.read_csv. Code that starts with from imports specific functions from the given libraries, making them available without needing to prefix them with the library name. The asterisk statements imports all the functions from the corresponding packages so you don’t have to import each one individually. The second-to-last line imports the col function from Polars and aliases it as c. We will use this function extensively throughout the book; the shorthand will greatly reduce clutter in our code. The final line loads all of the custom functions in a local file called funs.py. These are wrapper functions we have created to help simplify the code in this text. Each wrapper will be explained as they arise and can be directly inspected by opening the Python script directly. Other modules, such as NumPy, will be imported in later chapters as soon as we need them.

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.8 Loading Data in Python
In this book, we will be primarily working with data stored in a tabular format. Fig. 1.2 shows a small excerpt of the country dataset that we will use as a running example throughout this chapter. The figure shows structures organized by rows and columns. Each row of the dataset represents a particular country; we call each row an observation. The columns represent the measurements we record for each observation; these measurements are called variables.






Figure 1.2: An example of a tabular dataset.



The excerpt shown in the figure includes five of these variables: the country’s name, the region of the world in which it is located, its population (in millions), its population density (in tens of thousands of people per square kilometer), and the median age of its residents. The full dataset contains additional variables beyond these five; more details are given in the following section.
A common format for storing tabular datasets are in plaintext comma-separated values (CSV) files. Almost all of the datasets in this book will use this format for storing the data that we will work with. To read a dataset into Python, we use the function pl.read_csv() from the Polars library. We call pl.read_csv() with the path to the file relative to the script’s location. Below is an example of how to read the dataset contained in the file “data/countries.csv” and save it as an object called country. The resulting dataset is stored as a Python object called a DataFrame.

country = pl.read_csv("data/countries.csv")

Notice that the display shows a total of 135 rows and 15 columns. Or, with our terms defined above, there are 135 observations and 15 variables. Only the first five and last five observations are shown in the output, along with information about the shape of the DataFrame.
The data types shown by Polars, such as str, i64, and f64, tell us the type of data stored in each column. Understanding these types is important enough that we devote the next chapter to them.

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.9 Formatting Code
It is important to format Python code consistently. Even if code runs without errors and produces the desired results, consistent formatting makes it easier to read, debug, and maintain. Throughout this book, we will follow the conventions below, based on PEP 8 (Python’s official style guide). These are the same conventions used by professional data scientists and software developers. Adopt these rules whenever you write code for this course.

Use one space before and after the equals sign = and around binary operators (such as +, -, *, /, %, <, >, ==).
Exception: do not use spaces around the equals sign when specifying function arguments or keyword arguments (for example, def f(x=5): or geom_text(size=6)).
Use one space after a comma, but no space before it.
Use lowercase letters with underscores for variable and function names (snake_case).
Use double quotes for strings (for example, "hello" rather than 'hello').
Keep lines reasonably short; if a line becomes too long (typically over 79–88 characters), split it across multiple lines.
When breaking code across multiple lines:

Wrap the expression in parentheses
Indent continuation lines by 4 spaces
Put each logical step (method call, operator) on its own line
For operator chaining, place the operator at the start of the continuation line
Align the closing parenthesis with the start of the expression

Avoid trailing whitespace and keep indentation consistent throughout the file.

The following example applies these formatting rules in practice. Do not worry if you have not yet seen these specific functions or libraries; we will cover them later in the course. For now, focus on the structure and layout of the code.

(
    country
    .filter(c.region == "Europe")
    .pipe(ggplot, aes("hdi", "happy"))
    + geom_point()
    + geom_text(aes(label="iso"), size=6, nudge_y=0.5)
)

Notice how spacing, indentation, and line breaks work together to make the logic clear and readable. Following these conventions from the start will make your work easier to read, debug, and maintain.

title: 1  Introduction
text:
1.10 Extensions
Each chapter in this book contains a short concluding section with extensions to the main material. These include references for further study, additional Python libraries, and other suggested methods that may be of interest for the study of each specific type of humanities data.
In this chapter, we mention a few standard Python references that may be useful to consult alongside our text. The classic introduction to the Python language is Learning Python by Mark Lutz [8]. For those specifically interested in data science applications, Python for Data Analysis by Wes McKinney (the creator of pandas) provides comprehensive coverage of the core data science libraries [7].
For the specific approach to data analysis we follow in this book, Python Data Science Handbook by Jake VanderPlas is an excellent reference [9]. It covers the full stack of data science tools in Python, from basic data manipulation through machine learning. The book is also freely available online.
For those interested in the grammar of graphics approach to visualization that we use throughout this book, The Grammar of Graphics by Leland Wilkinson provides the theoretical foundation [10]. The plotnine module implements these concepts in Python, closely following the ggplot2 implementation in R.

title: 1  Introduction
text:

[2] Brillinger, D R (2002 ). John w. Tukey: His life and professional contributions. Annals of Statistics. JSTOR. 1535–75


[3] Becker, R A and Chambers, J M (1984 ). S: An Interactive Environment for Data Analysis and Graphics. CRC Press


[4] Becker, R A and Chambers, J M (1985 ). Extending the S System. Wadsworth Advanced Books; Software


[5] Becker, R A, Chambers, J M and Wilks, A R (1988 ). The New S Language: A Programming Environment for Data Analysis and Graphics. Wadsworth Advanced Books; Software


[6] Chambers, J M and Hastie, T J (1991 ). Statistical Models in S. CRC Press


[7] McKinney, W (2012 ). Python for Data Analysis: Data Wrangling with Pandas, NumPy, and IPython. " O’Reilly Media, Inc."


[8] Lutz, M (2013 ). Learning Python: Powerful Object-Oriented Programming. " O’Reilly Media, Inc."


[9] VanderPlas, J, Granger, B, Heer, J, Moritz, D, Wongsuphasawat, K, Satyanarayan, A, Lees, E, Timofeev, I, Welsh, B and Sievert, S (2018 ). Altair: Interactive statistical visualizations for python. Journal of open source software. The Open Journal. 3 1057


[10] Wilkinson, L (2012 ). The Grammar of Graphics. Springer

title: 2  Data Types and Expressions
text:
2.1 Setup
As in every chapter, we begin by loading the modules and datasets used below. This chapter needs Polars, our helper functions, and the country dataset from Chapter 1. A second, smaller dataset will be read partway through the chapter, at the point where it becomes useful.
import polars as pl
from plotnine import ggplot, aes
from polars import col as c

import funs

country = pl.read_csv("data/countries.csv")

title: 2  Data Types and Expressions
text:
2.2 Introduction
At the end of the previous chapter, we read the country dataset into Python and printed it. Under each column name, the display showed a short label: str under the country names, i64 under the population counts, f64 under the latitudes. These labels are data types. Every column in a DataFrame has exactly one type, and that type determines what the column can do: whether its values can be added together, whether they sort numerically or alphabetically, and how missing entries behave.
This chapter has two goals. The first is to explain the type system: what the common types are, how to check them, how to convert a column from one type to another, and what happens when a value is missing or invalid. The second goal is quieter but pays off for the rest of the book. Expressions such as c.pop look like they refer to data, but they are actually small descriptions of computations that have not happened yet. Understanding this distinction explains why the same c. syntax appears inside so many different methods in the chapters ahead.

title: 2  Data Types and Expressions
text:
2.3 Columns Have Types
Printing a DataFrame shows the type of each column directly under its name. To see the full mapping at once, we can ask for the DataFrame’s schema, which lists every column name alongside its type.

country.schema

Schema([('iso', String),
        ('full_name', String),
        ('region', String),
        ('subregion', String),
        ('pop', Float64),
        ('lexp', Float64),
        ('lat', Float64),
        ('lon', Float64),
        ('hdi', Float64),
        ('gdp', Int64),
        ('gini', Float64),
        ('happy', Float64),
        ('cellphone', Float64),
        ('water_access', Float64),
        ('lang', String)])


Four types cover almost everything in this book. A str (string) column holds text, such as the country names and regions. An i64 (64-bit integer) column holds whole numbers. An f64 (64-bit floating point) column holds numbers that may have a decimal part, such as latitude or the Human Development Index. The fourth type, bool (Boolean), holds only the values True and False. There is no bool column in country as it is read from the file, but one appears as soon as we ask a yes-or-no question about the data.
To display a computed column, we can use the .select() method, which builds a DataFrame out of whatever columns and computations we give it. We will study .select() properly in Chapter 3; here we only need it to look at results. The code below shows each country’s HDI next to a test of whether that HDI is above 0.9.

(
    country
    .select(c.iso, c.hdi, high_hdi = c.hdi > 0.9)
)

The new column’s header reads bool. Comparison operators (>, <, >=, <=, ==, !=) always produce Boolean columns, whatever the type of their inputs. These Boolean columns will do a lot of work in the next chapter, where they decide which rows of a dataset to keep.

title: 2  Data Types and Expressions
text:
2.4 Why Types Matter
The country data arrives in good shape: numbers are read as numbers and text as text, so there is little reason to think about types at all. Most real datasets are not this polite. To see what goes wrong, we will read a second file, a small table of Massachusetts cities with their ZIP codes, populations, and founding dates.

cities = pl.read_csv("data/cities_messy.csv")
cities


shape: (8, 4)



city
zip
pop
founded


str
i64
str
str




"Agawam"
1001
"28613"
"1855-05-17"


"Amherst"
1002
"39263"
"1759-02-13"


"Boston"
2108
"675647"
"1630-09-07"


"Lowell"
1852
"115554"
"1826-03-01"


"Pittsfield"
1201
"n/a"
"1761-04-21"


"Salem"
1970
"44480"
"1626-06-24"


"Springfield"
1103
"155929"
"1636-05-14"


"Worcester"
1608
"206518"
"1722-06-14"






Look at the printed types. The zip column was read as i64, and every code has lost its leading zero: Boston’s ZIP code is 02108, but the table says 2108. ZIP codes are labels that happen to be written with digits, not quantities, and treating them as integers has silently damaged them. The pop column has the opposite problem. One city’s population was entered as "n/a", and because a column must have a single type, that one entry forced the entire column to be read as str. The founded column is also str, since read_csv has no way to know those particular strings are dates.
None of this produces an error message. The file reads, the table prints, and everything looks roughly right. The damage only surfaces when we try to use the data. Suppose we ask for the largest population in the table:

(
    cities
    .select(c.pop.max())
)


shape: (1, 1)



pop


str




"n/a"






The answer is "n/a". Strings compare alphabetically, character by character, and "n" comes after every digit. Even without the stray entry, alphabetical order would put "28613" after "206518", because the comparison never gets past the first character. Arithmetic fails more loudly: asking for c.pop * 2 raises an error, since Polars refuses to multiply text. A column with the wrong type cannot be trusted, and the printed header is where the problem shows up first, so checking it after every read_csv is worth making a habit.

title: 2  Data Types and Expressions
text:
2.5 Casting
Converting a column from one type to another is called casting, done with the .cast() method on a column. The target type is written with a pl. prefix and a capitalized name: pl.Int64, pl.Float64, pl.String, and pl.Boolean correspond to the four header labels we have seen. These longer names are how Polars refers to types inside code, while the headers use short forms to save space.
Casting between the numeric types works as you might expect, with one caution. Converting an integer column to pl.Float64 is always safe. Going the other way, from floating point to pl.Int64, drops the decimal part entirely rather than rounding. The example below casts latitude to an integer; a latitude of 41.9 becomes 41, and -34.6 becomes -34.

(
    country
    .select(c.iso, c.lat, lat_int = c.lat.cast(pl.Int64))
)

Now back to the broken pop column in cities. Casting it to a numeric type should fix it, but a plain cast fails, because "n/a" cannot be turned into a number. Polars raises an error rather than guessing. To convert the column anyway, we add strict=False, which tells the cast to replace any value it cannot convert with a missing value.

(
    cities
    .select(
      c.city,
      pop = c.pop.cast(pl.Int64, strict=False)
    )
)

The column is now i64, the real populations are numbers, and Pittsfield’s entry has become null, the subject of the next section. Its maximum is now a number rather than "n/a".
What about the ZIP codes? Casting c.zip to pl.String turns 2108 back into text, but the text it produces is "2108"; the leading zero was discarded when the file was read and a cast cannot recover it. The honest fix is to prevent the damage in the first place by telling read_csv to treat the column as text from the start. We cover the options for reading imperfect files in Chapter 10. In the meantime, the lesson stands: casting repairs types, not lost information.

title: 2  Data Types and Expressions
text:
2.6 Missing and Invalid Values
The cast above introduced a null into the pop column. A null is Polars’ marker for a missing value, and any column of any type can contain one. Floating point columns can additionally contain a second special value, NaN (short for “not a number”), which is not a missing value but the result of a numerical computation that has no answer. Taking the square root of a negative number is the classic way to produce one:

(
    country
    .select(c.iso, c.lat, lat_sqrt = c.lat.sqrt())
)


shape: (135, 3)



iso
lat
lat_sqrt


str
f64
f64




"SEN"
14.366667
3.790339


"VEN"
8.0
2.828427


"FIN"
65.0
8.062258


"USA"
39.828175
6.310957


"LKA"
7.0
2.645751


…
…
…


"ALB"
41.0
6.403124


"MYS"
3.7805111
1.944354


"SLV"
13.668889
3.697146


"CYP"
35.0
5.91608


"PAK"
30.0
5.477226






Countries in the Southern Hemisphere have negative latitudes, so their rows show NaN. Many libraries blur nulls and NaNs together; Polars keeps them separate, and it pays to keep the distinction straight. A null means “we do not have this value.” A NaN means “we computed this value and the mathematics broke.” The two are handled by parallel sets of tools: .is_null() tests for missing values and .is_nan() for invalid ones, .fill_null() replaces missing values and .fill_nan() replaces invalid ones, and the row-dropping methods .drop_nulls() and .drop_nans() mentioned in the next chapter remove rows containing each. Dropping nulls does not remove NaNs, and dropping NaNs does not remove nulls.
The filling methods take the replacement value as their argument. Below, we fill the missing population with 0. Whether 0 is a sensible stand-in depends entirely on what the analysis does next; for a sum it is harmless, while for an average it would quietly drag the result down. There is no universally correct fill value.

(
    cities
    .select(
      c.city,
      pop = c.pop.cast(pl.Int64, strict=False).fill_null(0)
    )
)


shape: (8, 2)



city
pop


str
i64




"Agawam"
28613


"Amherst"
39263


"Boston"
675647


"Lowell"
115554


"Pittsfield"
0


"Salem"
44480


"Springfield"
155929


"Worcester"
206518






Nulls also propagate: almost any arithmetic involving a null produces another null. Adding 5 to a missing value yields a missing value, which is usually what you want, since the alternative would be inventing data. Comparisons behave the same way, so a test like c.pop > 100 gives null, not False, for a missing population. This matters when Boolean columns are used to select rows in the next chapter: rows where the test comes out null are not kept. Aggregations are the exception to propagation. Methods such as .sum() and .mean() skip nulls rather than being poisoned by them, a design choice that is convenient right up until you forget how many values were skipped.
Polars also provides methods that fill a missing value using its neighbors in the column, copying the entry above or below it. These make the most sense for data with a meaningful row order, and we return to them in Chapter 6 alongside the other order-aware tools.

title: 2  Data Types and Expressions
text:
2.7 Expressions
We have now written things like c.hdi > 0.9 and c.pop.cast(pl.Int64) several times, always inside a call to .select(). It is time to ask what these fragments actually are. Try typing one alone in a cell:

c.pop

col("pop")


The result is not a column of numbers. It is an object called an expression, and its printed form is essentially a note that says “the column named pop.” No data is attached, because c.pop on its own is not connected to any DataFrame. It is a description of a computation, not the computation’s result.
Expressions can be built up using arithmetic, comparisons, and methods, and each step produces another expression. All of the following are expressions, and running any of them alone would again print a description rather than data:

c.pop * 1000
c.pop.sqrt()
c.hdi > 0.9
c.pop.cast(pl.Int64, strict=False).fill_null(0)

An expression only produces values when it is handed to a context: a DataFrame method that applies the description to actual data. We have used one context so far, .select(), which evaluates expressions and returns their results as columns. A second is .with_columns(), which evaluates expressions and attaches the results to the existing DataFrame as new columns:

(
    country
    .with_columns(pop_1k = c.pop * 1000)
)

Notice that the expression c.hdi > 0.9 is identical in spirit to the one we put in .select() earlier in the chapter. The expression itself does not know or care which context will run it. This is the reason the c. syntax repeats across the whole book. The next chapter uses expressions inside .sort(), .select(), and .filter(); Chapter 5 uses the same expressions inside .agg() to summarize groups; Chapter 6 uses them inside .over() to compute within groups. The syntax is worth learning carefully now, because nothing about it changes later.
The separation between describing a computation and running it has a practical benefit as well. Because a chain of methods hands Polars a full description of the work before any of it starts, Polars can reorganize the steps internally to run them faster. Nothing needs to change in your code for this to happen; it is simply a consequence of the design.






NoteColumn names that break the c. syntax





Writing c.pop only works when the column name is a valid Python name: letters, digits, and underscores, not starting with a digit. A column called median age or GDP (2020) cannot follow the dot. For these, call c as a function with the name in quotes, as in c("median age"). The resulting expression behaves exactly like any other. If a dataset will be used at length, it is often easier to rename such columns once at the start; the .rename() method appears in the next chapter.

title: 2  Data Types and Expressions
text:
2.8 Strings, Dates, and Categories
The four core types are not the whole story, and this section previews two families of tools that get fuller treatment later.
String columns carry a collection of methods under the .str prefix, covering tasks like changing case, measuring length, and extracting pieces of text. One small example, converting names to uppercase:

(
    country
    .select(c.iso, name_upper = c.full_name.str.to_uppercase())
)

Dates get their own types as well. The founded column in cities is currently a str, which means the values can be printed but not treated as points in time; nothing relates "1626-06-24" to "1630-09-07" except alphabetical order, which for dates written year-first happens to coincide with time order, and for most other formats does not. The .str.to_date() method parses text into a genuine date column:

(
    cities
    .select(c.city, founded = c.founded.str.to_date())
)


shape: (8, 2)



city

A date column knows about years, months, and durations, and supports arithmetic that respects the calendar. We will use dates when we meet time-based data later in the book.
Finally, Polars offers a Categorical type for string columns that take values from a fixed, limited set, such as region in the country data. It stores each distinct value once, which saves memory on large datasets and can make grouping faster. For the data sizes in this book the plain str type is simpler and entirely adequate, so we mention Categorical only so it is recognizable in documentation and in other people’s code.

title: 2  Data Types and Expressions
text:
2.9 Extensions
The full list of Polars data types, including several this chapter skipped (unsigned and smaller-width integers, Datetime, Duration, List, and others), is documented in the data types section of the Polars user guide. The guide’s page on expressions and contexts covers the same ground as this chapter’s second half in more technical depth, and is worth reading once the ideas here feel comfortable. Readers curious about why Polars separates null from NaN can look up the Apache Arrow memory format, which Polars builds on and which makes the missing-value marker essentially free.

title: 3  Organizing Data I
text:
3.1 Setup
We will begin this and every subsequent chapter by listing the core modules and datasets that must be loaded before getting started. Datasets and sources that require new functions will be loaded within the body of the text.
import polars as pl
from plotnine import ggplot, aes
from polars import col as c

import funs

country = pl.read_csv("data/countries.csv")

title: 3  Organizing Data I
text:
3.2 Introduction
This chapter covers the core methods for organizing a dataset: reordering its rows, choosing which columns to keep, and filtering down to the rows you care about. We will also look at how to create new columns from existing ones, rename columns, and remove duplicate rows. Every method in this chapter works on a single DataFrame at a time, one row or one column at a time, with no notion of groups. Later chapters build on these same methods once we start comparing categories of data against each other.
All of the operations below come from the Polars library, the same one we used to read our data. Each takes a DataFrame as input and returns a modified DataFrame as output, which means the methods can be chained together in a single pipeline. Individually, these methods are simple. Chained together, they let you turn a raw dataset into exactly the shape you need for an analysis.

title: 3  Organizing Data I
text:
3.3 Sorting Rows
We use the .sort() method to reorder the rows of a dataset according to one or more columns. This is useful for finding extreme values or scanning a dataset in a specific order. Let’s sort the countries dataset by population and then walk through how the code works.

(
    country
    .sort(c.pop)
)

A few things to note in this code. The code is wrapped in a pair of parentheses, each on its own line, with every other line indented by four spaces. The dataset sits on its own line, followed by the sort method on the next. We use the c. prefix followed by the column name to refer to a column. The result is printed, but the original dataset is unchanged: after running this code, country still has its original row order. These conventions carry over to every DataFrame method in this chapter.
You can reverse the sort order by setting the descending parameter to True.

(
    country
    .sort(c.pop, descending=True)
)

To keep a sorted dataset for later use, store it in a named variable:

country_saved = (
    country
    .sort(c.region, c.pop)
)

After running this code, country_saved holds the reordered dataset, while country still holds the original. The same pattern applies to the results of any method described in this chapter.

title: 3  Organizing Data I
text:
3.4 Selecting Columns
When a dataset has many columns, it is often useful to select or reorder a subset of them. Use the .select method with the same c. notation to specify which columns to keep.

(
    country
    .select(c.iso, c.lat, c.lon)
)

We will use .select throughout this text to point to the specific columns worth examining. It matters less in a typical data science pipeline, but it becomes important for more complex transformations, such as those in Chapter 9.
A related method, .drop, lets us specify columns to remove instead. This is handy when a dataset has many columns and only a few need to go.

title: 3  Organizing Data I
text:
3.5 Selecting Rows
We often want a subset of a dataset’s rows for a particular analysis or visualization. Which function to use depends on the selection criteria. The .head() method returns a specified number of rows from the top of the dataset. The example below selects the first four rows.

(
    country
    .head(n=4)
)

We can filter categorical variables with equality comparisons too. To keep the page from getting too cluttered, we will stop printing the result of every example from here on.

(
    country
    .filter(c.region == "Europe")
)

This keeps every row where region == "Europe". Note the double-equals operator (==) for testing equality; a single equals sign (=) is reserved for assignment.
We can combine multiple conditions with the & (and) and | (or) operators. Wrap each condition in parentheses when doing so:

(
    country
    .filter((c.region == "Europe") & (c.hdi > 0.9))
)

To check whether a value belongs to a larger set, use the .is_in method, passing the set of values in square brackets.

(
    country
    .filter(c.region.is_in(["Europe", "Africa"]))
)

title: 3  Organizing Data I
text:
3.6 Rename and Unique
Two smaller methods round out this set of tools: .rename() and .unique().
Use .rename() to give one or more columns a new name, passing a dictionary that maps each old name to its replacement. This is useful when a column name is unclear, inconsistent with the rest of your naming scheme, or awkward to type.

(
    country
    .rename({"pop": "population"})
)

title: 3  Organizing Data I
text:
3.7 Modifying Columns
Creating or modifying columns is a fundamental part of data analysis. You will often need to compute a new variable from existing columns, build an indicator (dummy) variable, or transform a variable before analysis.
The basic pattern for adding a column is the .with_columns() method: specify each new or modified value, defined in terms of existing columns. The example below multiplies the pop column (in millions) by 1,000 to get the population in thousands.

(
    country
    .with_columns(population_1k = c.pop * 1000)
)

Common mathematical transformations are available by adding a . after the column name, followed by a function such as sqrt() or log10().
The following example takes the square root and the sine of population and saves each as a new column. Neither transformation is meaningful here; they are only meant to show the technique.

(
    country
    .with_columns(
      pop_sqrt = c.pop.sqrt(),
      pop_sin = c.pop.sin()
    )
)

Note the formatting: when a column definition is long, or when defining several columns at once, put each one on its own line, indented by four spaces.






NoteAlias method





Polars includes a method called .alias that can be used within .with_columns to create or overwrite a column. It was the original standard technique but has largely been replaced by the equals-sign syntax used above, which most people find more intuitive. It’s worth recognizing because you will still see it often in the Polars documentation.

title: 3  Organizing Data I
text:
3.8 Pipes
Chaining methods together is a powerful pattern. It keeps code readable, avoids naming a pile of intermediate variables, and lets Polars optimize the whole sequence of operations internally. One limitation of method chaining is that it only gives you access to methods built into Polars. The .pipe method gets around this by letting you call an arbitrary function with the DataFrame as its first argument. .pipe takes the function to apply as its first argument, and passes any additional arguments straight through to that function.
As a simple example, we can apply the helper function print_rows to a DataFrame to control how many rows are shown, instead of the default 10. Here we select 4, which shows the first two rows and the last two.

(
    country
    .print(4)
)

title: 3  Organizing Data I
text:
3.9 Further Functions
One more function is worth knowing before moving on: pl.when, which creates a column that depends on a condition elsewhere in the data. The example below builds a column equal to 1 for countries in the Northern Hemisphere and 0 for those in the Southern Hemisphere.

(
    country
    .with_columns(
        is_north = pl.when(c.lat >= 0).then(1).otherwise(0)
    )
)

pl.when can chain additional .when().then() pairs before the final .otherwise(), which makes it a compact way to build a column out of several conditions at once.

title: 3  Organizing Data I
text:
3.10 R’s dplyr and Python Pandas
If you’re familiar with dplyr in R or pandas in Python, the code in this chapter should feel familiar. Function names differ, and each library has its own quirks, but the underlying task is the same: compose a set of methods to transform data into the shape a given analysis needs.
Coming from dplyr, the main adjustments are prefixing column names with c., using methods instead of pipes (the same idea, different syntax), and a few renamed functions—sort in place of arrange, for instance.
Coming from Pandas involves a few more changes, most of which simplify the code. There is no index column to reset after every operation, no aggregation functions wrapped in tuples, and no eval or query methods buried behind an extra layer of quoted strings.
As with Pandas and dplyr, Polars also provides functions for pivots and table joins, which we cover in Chapter 9. The arguments differ slightly across the three libraries, but the underlying transformations are much the same.

title: 4  Visualizing Data I
text:
4.1 Setup
Load all of the modules and datasets needed for the chapter.
import polars as pl
from plotnine import ggplot, aes
from polars import col as c

import funs

country = pl.read_csv("data/countries.csv")
cellphone = pl.read_csv("data/countries_cellphone.csv")

title: 4  Visualizing Data I
text:
4.2 Introduction
In this chapter, we will learn and use the plotnine package for building informative graphics [1] [2]. The package makes it easy to build fairly complex graphics guided by a general theory of data visualization. The only downside is that, because it is built around a theoretical model rather than many one-off solutions for different tasks, it has a somewhat steeper initial learning curve. The chapter is designed to get us started using the package to make a variety of data visualizations.
The core idea of the grammar of graphics is that visualizations are composed of independent layers. The term “grammar” is used because the theory builds connections between elements of the dataset and elements of a visualization. It assembles complex elements from smaller ones, much like a grammar relates words to generate larger phrases and sentences. To describe a specific layer, we need to specify several components. First, we must specify the dataset from which data will be taken to construct the plot. Next, we specify a set of mappings called aesthetics that describe how elements of the plot relate to columns in the data. For example, we often indicate which column corresponds to the horizontal axis and which corresponds to the vertical axis. We can also describe attributes such as color, shape, and size by associating these quantities with columns in the data. Finally, we need to provide the geometry that will be used in the plot. The geometry describes the kinds of objects associated with each row of the data. A common example is the points geometry, which associates a single point with each observation.
Every plot in this chapter is built by piping a dataset into ggplot, so this chapter assumes you have already worked through Chapter 3 on pipes. What we cover here are geometries that draw the rows of a dataset directly: one point, label, line segment, or bar per row. Some of the most useful plots in practice — histograms, boxplots, density curves, trend lines — compute something from the data before drawing it, such as a count, a percentile, or a fitted line. Those wait until Chapter 7, once we know how Polars computes group summaries; that chapter also covers facets, scales, and the finer points of labeling and theming a plot.

title: 4  Visualizing Data I
text:
4.3 Points Geometry
We can demonstrate the grammar of graphics by using the countries data, where each row corresponds to a particular country. The first plot we will make is a scatter plot that investigates the relationship between the Human Development Index (HDI)—a “composite index of life expectancy, education (mean years of schooling completed and expected years of schooling upon entering the education system), and per capita income indicators, which is used to rank countries into four tiers of human development”—and perceived happiness. In the language of the grammar of graphics, we begin describing this visualization by specifying the Python variable containing the data (country). Next, we associate the horizontal axis (the x aesthetic) with the column in the data named hdi. The vertical axis (the y aesthetic) can similarly be associated with the column named happy. We will make a scatter plot, with each point describing one of our countries, which leads us to use a point geometry. Our plot will allow us to understand the relationship between human development and happiness.
In Python, we need to use some special functions to indicate all of this information and to instruct the program to produce a plot. We start by specifying the name of the underlying dataset and applying the special method .pipe with its first argument set to the function ggplot. This indicates that we want to create a data visualization. In the second argument of that function we use the aes (short for aesthetics) function to specify the names of the x and y aesthetics corresponding to columns in the dataset. The plot itself is created by adding—literally, with the plus sign—the geom_point function. This function adds a point geometry (a layer of points) to the plot. Code to do this using the values described above is given below.

(
    country
    .filter(c.region == "Europe")
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.hdi, c.happy))
    .geom_point()
)














NoteChoosing x and y aesthetics





When we have a scatter plot showing the relationship between two columns in a dataset, the usual convention is to put the quantity that is more likely to be a cause on the x-axis and the variable more likely to be the effect or outcome on the y-axis. Here, we put the HDI on the x-axis because it seems more likely that investment in human development leads to happier people rather than the other way around. This general rule can be broken when needed to make the plot more readable or to make it easier to compare with other related visualizations.



Running the code above will show the desired visualization directly below the code block. In this plot, each row of our dataset — a country — is represented as a point. The location of each point is determined by the country’s HDI and happiness score. Notice that Python has automatically made several choices for the plot that we did not explicitly indicate in the code. For example, the range of values on the two axes, the axis labels, the grid lines, and the tick marks. Python has also automatically picked the color, size, and shape of the points. While the defaults are a good starting point, it’s often useful to modify them; we will see how to change these aspects of the plot in later sections of this chapter.
As shown in the example, we can include data-manipulation code before the call to .pipe. This lets us seamlessly combine data manipulation and data visualization.
Scatter plots are typically used to examine the relationship between two numeric variables. What does our first plot tell us about the relationship between HDI and happiness? It appears that, at least in Europe, there is a generally positive relationship between investment in human development and the reported happiness of a population.

title: 4  Visualizing Data I
text:
4.4 Optional Aesthetics
The point geometry requires x and y aesthetics. In addition to these required aesthetics, each geometry type also has several optional aesthetics that add information to the plot. For example, most geometries have a color aesthetic. The syntax for specifying this is similar to the required aesthetics, with the only difference being that we place the aesthetics inside the geom_point() function using another call to aes(). This is true for all aesthetics other than x and y. Let’s see what happens when we add a color aesthetic to our scatter plot by mapping the column subregion to the color aesthetic.

(
    country
    .filter(c.region == "Europe")
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.hdi, c.happy))
    .geom_point(aes(color=c.subregion))
)








The result of associating a column in the dataset with a color produces a new variation of the original scatter plot. We have the same set of points and locations on the plot, as well as the same axes. However, now each subregion has been automatically associated with a color, and every point has been colored according to the subregion value in each row of the data. The mapping between colors and region names is shown in an automatically created legend on the right-hand side of the plot. The ability to add additional information to the plot by specifying a single aesthetic mapping illustrates how powerful the grammar of graphics is for quickly producing informative visualizations.
In the previous example, we changed the color aesthetic from the fixed default color of black to a color that varies with another variable. It is also possible to specify an alternative fixed value for any aesthetic. We can use the color names available in Python; for example, we might want all of the points to be a shade of green. This can be done with a small change to the function call: set the color aesthetic to the name of a color, such as “red”. However, unlike with variable aesthetics, the mapping needs to be done outside of the aes() function but still within the geom_* function. Below is an example of the code to recreate our plot with a different color. We use a color called “#F5276C”, which is a bright reddish-pink.

(
    country
    .filter(c.region == "Europe")
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.hdi, c.happy))
    .geom_point(color="#F5276C")
)














NoteHexadecimal color codes





The color code below uses a format called an HTML hexadecimal color code. These codes let us unambiguously describe a large number of colors with a single string. The first two characters specify the amount of red, the next two the amount of green, and the last two the amount of blue. The values are written in base-16 (hexadecimal) notation, where the digits a–f correspond to the numbers 10 through 15. Usually, people use an interactive website to determine the hex code for a desired color. One good choice is htmlcolorcodes.



Although minor, the change in notation for specifying fixed aesthetics is a common source of confusion for users new to the grammar of graphics; follow the correct argument syntax shown in the code above. You can interchange the fixed and variable aesthetic commands; the relative order should not affect the output. Just be sure to put fixed terms after finishing the aes() call.
While each geometry may have different required and optional aesthetics, the plotnine package tries, as much as possible, to use a common set of terms across geometries. Here are some typical aesthetics we’ll see for most geometries:

color: the color of points, lines, or the insides of boxes
fill: the interior color of boxes and polygons
alpha: controls the opacity of objects; set to a number between 0 and 1
size: controls the size of the points, boxes, font etc. of the layer
shape: controls the shape of the points
group: groups together data without changing the color; useful for lines

Some of these, such as alpha, are most frequently used with fixed values. If needed, however, almost all can be provided with a variable mapping.

title: 4  Visualizing Data I
text:
4.5 Text Geometry
A common critique of computational methods is that they obscure close understanding of each individual object of study by focusing on numeric patterns. This is an important caution: computational analysis should be paired with close analysis. However, visualizations do not always have to reduce complex collections to a few numerical summaries, especially when the dataset contains a relatively small number of observations. Looking back at our first scatter plot, how could we recover a closer analysis of individual countries while also looking for general patterns between the two variables? One option is to add labels indicating the names of the countries. These labels allow viewers to bring their own knowledge of the countries as an additional layer of information when interpreting the plot.
Adding the names of the countries can be done by using another type of geometry, called text geometry. This geometry is created with the function geom_text. For each row of a dataset, this geometry adds a small textual label. As with the point geometry, we must specify which columns of our data correspond to the x and y aesthetics; these values tell the plot where to place the label. Additionally, the text geometry requires an aesthetic called label that provides the text for each label. In our case, we will use the column called iso to make textual labels on the plot based on the three-letter ISO abbreviation of the country. The code block below produces a plot with text labels by changing the geometry type and adding the label aesthetic.

(
    country
    .filter(c.region == "Europe")
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.hdi, c.happy))
    .geom_text(aes(label=c.iso))
) 








The plot generated by the code now allows us to see which countries have the highest levels of happiness (Finland, Iceland, and Denmark) and which have the highest levels of HDI (Iceland, Norway, and Switzerland (CHE)). While we added only a single additional piece of information to the plot, each label uniquely identifies a row of the data. This allows anyone familiar with them to bring many more characteristics of each data point to the plot through their own knowledge. For example, although the plot does not include any information about geography, anyone familiar with Europe will note that the Scandinavian countries are clustered together in the upper-right corner of the plot.
Although the text plot adds contextual information compared with the scatter plot, it has some shortcomings. Some labels overlap and become difficult to read, and these issues will only worsen if we increase the number of regions in our dataset. It is also unclear which part of a label corresponds to a country’s HDI or happiness score: the center, the start, or the end? We could add a note that the value refers to the center of the label, but that becomes cumbersome to remember and to keep reminding others about.
To start addressing these issues, we can add the points back into the plot, with labels. We do this in Python by adding the two geometry layers (geom_point and geom_text) one after the other. This makes it clearer where each region is located on the x-axis, but it also makes the country names harder to read. To fix that, we’ll add options to the text geometry to nudge the labels upward and reduce their size. Below is the code that makes both of these modifications.

(
    country
    .filter(c.region == "Europe")
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.hdi, c.happy))
    .geom_point()
    .geom_text(aes(label=c.iso), size=6, nudge_y=0.5)
) 








The plot with points and text labels shows that we attempted to avoid overlapping labels by nudging them slightly. The points indicate the specific HDI and happiness scores, while the labels identify which country each point represents.

title: 4  Visualizing Data I
text:
4.6 Lines and Bars
The plotnine package supplies a large number of different geometries. In this section, we will look at two additional geometry types that allow us to investigate common relationships among the columns of a dataset.
For a moment, we look at the cellphone dataset that shows the number of cellphones per one-hundred people over a twenty-year period for each country. Consider a visualization showing the change in the number of cellphones over time. We could create a scatter plot where each point represents a row of the data, the x aesthetic captures the year of each record, and the y aesthetic measures the number of phones. This visualization would be fine and would roughly help us understand changes in cellphone ownership. A more common visualization for data of this format, however, is a line plot, where the values in each year are connected by a line to the values in the subsequent year. To create such a plot, we can use the geom_line geometry. It is most commonly used when the horizontal axis measures some unit of time but can represent other quantities that we expect to change continuously and smoothly between measurements on the x-axis. The line geometry uses the same aesthetics as the point geometry and can be created with the same syntax, as shown in the following block of code.

(
    cellphone
    .filter(c.iso.is_in(["USA", "GBR", "IRL", "CAN"]))
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.year, c.cell))
    .geom_line(aes(color=c.iso))
)








The output of this visualization shows how the number of cell phones changes over time. The numbers generally increase slowly in North American countries. In Great Britain, they plateau near 2010, while in Ireland they decrease slightly from a peak around 2007.
Another common use of visualization is to compare the values of a numeric column with the categories of a categorical column. You can represent this relationship with a geom_point layer, but it is often more meaningful visually to use a bar for each category, with the height or length of the bar representing the numeric value. This type of plot is common for showing counts of categories (something we will see in a later chapter), but it can be used whenever a numeric value is associated with different categories. To create a plot with bars, we use the geom_col function, providing both x and y aesthetics. By default the categorical variable maps to x and the numeric variable maps to y, but it is often easier to read the other way around. To address this, we add the coord_flip() layer, which flips the axes. The code block below shows the population of each country in Northern Africa, a subregion with few enough categories that the default alphabetical ordering does not get in the way.

(
    country
    .filter(c.subregion == "Northern Africa")
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.full_name, c.pop))
    .geom_col()
    .coord_flip()
)








With only a handful of countries, reading down an alphabetized list of bars is no real burden. That stops being true once a category has dozens of members. Chapter 7 revisits this same geometry with a much longer list of categories and introduces the reorder() function, which sorts the bars by their values instead of their names.

title: 4  Visualizing Data I
text:
4.7 Multiple Datasets
So far, we have seen how to create visualizations using multiple layers with a single dataset. It is also possible to use different datasets in each layer by defining the data= argument for a specific geometry. When setting the data= argument for a geometry, it affects only that geometry; all other geometries will continue to use the default dataset.
A common reason to use two different datasets in the same plot is to highlight a subset of points within a larger dataset.

country_ne = country.filter(c.subregion == "Northern Europe")
(
    country
    .filter(c.region == "Europe")
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.hdi, c.happy))
    .geom_point(alpha=0.2)
    .geom_point(color="#F5276C", data=country_ne)
    .geom_text(
        aes(label=c.iso),
        color="#F5276C",
        nudge_y=0.5,
        size=8,
        data=country_ne
    ) 
)








This approach makes it easy to highlight the relationship between a subset of the data and the full dataset.

title: 4  Visualizing Data I
text:
4.8 A Note on Labels
Every plot in this chapter has used Python’s default axis labels: the raw column names from the dataset. That’s a deliberate choice rather than something we forgot to clean up. During exploratory analysis, the defaults are usually the right call. They cost no extra effort, and because they match the column names in your code, there’s no risk of a label drifting out of sync with the variable it describes. Once you’re ready to share a plot with someone else, you can override any of this with the labs() function, which lets you set a custom x, y, title, subtitle, caption, or legend title. We’ll start using labs() regularly in Chapter 7, once we’re building plots for an audience rather than for exploration.

title: 4  Visualizing Data I
text:
References




[1] Lavanya, A, Gaurav, L, Sindhuja, S, Seam, H, Joydeep, M, Uppalapati, V, Ali, W and SD, V S (2023 ). Assessing the performance of python data visualization libraries: A review. Int. J. Comput. Eng. Res. Trends. 10 28–39


[2] Wilkinson, L (2012 ). The Grammar of Graphics. Springer

title: 5  Grouping and Aggregation
text:
5.1 Setup
As in every chapter, we begin by loading the modules and datasets we need before doing anything else. Any dataset or function that is specific to a single example will be loaded later, in the body of the text.
import polars as pl
from plotnine import ggplot, aes
from polars import col as c

import funs

country = pl.read_csv("data/countries.csv")

title: 5  Grouping and Aggregation
text:
5.2 Introduction
So far we have worked one row at a time: sorting them, filtering them, and computing new columns from existing ones. This chapter is about the opposite move. Instead of asking a question of each row, we ask a question of a whole set of rows at once. What is the average life expectancy in each region? How many countries have a population above one hundred million? Which region has the widest spread in its Human Development Index?
Every question of this kind has two parts. First we decide how to split the rows into groups. Then we decide what single number to compute for each group. Polars keeps these two parts separate, and understanding why is most of the work in this chapter. The rest is a small vocabulary of functions for the second part, computing the number.

title: 5  Grouping and Aggregation
text:
5.3 Grouping Data
There is a method called .group_by(...) that groups a dataset by one or more variables, using a syntax much like .sort(...). When it appears in a method chain, the method that comes next is applied separately to each set of rows that share the same value or values for the grouping variables. For example, the following code selects the first country from each region in the dataset.

(
    country
    .group_by(c.region)
    .head(n=1)
)

The pattern of sorting and then taking the top row of each group comes up often; keep it in mind as a standard move. The sections that follow describe the other methods that can act on grouped data.

title: 5  Grouping and Aggregation
text:
5.4 What group_by Returns
Here is a question that looks trivial but is not. What happens if we call .group_by and stop there, without adding a method after it?

(
    country
    .group_by(c.region)
)

You might expect a table with the regions listed out, or perhaps the original data with some marker showing the groups. You get neither. Running this code prints a single line naming a GroupBy object along with a memory address, something with no rows and no columns to show. It is not a DataFrame at all.
This is the idea to hold onto for the rest of the chapter. In Polars, .group_by does not compute anything and does not return data. It returns a promise: an instruction that says “the next operation should run once per group.” That promise has to be consumed by whatever method comes next. The methods that can consume it are a short list: .agg(), which we turn to in the next section, along with .head(), .tail(), .len(), and a handful of direct shortcuts like .mean() and .sum() that aggregate every non-grouping column at once. A GroupBy object cannot be printed as a table because there is nothing to print until you say what to do with the groups. This is why the .head(n=1) examples above worked: the .head supplied the missing instruction.
If you are coming to Polars from R or from pandas, this will feel unfamiliar, and the reason is easy to miss.
In dplyr, group_by() returns a grouped tibble. The grouping is a property that sticks to the data. You can group once, run several verbs, and every one of them respects the grouping, right up until you call ungroup() to remove it. Forgetting to ungroup is a classic source of bugs precisely because the grouping persists silently.
In pandas, df.groupby(...) returns a DataFrameGroupBy object, and it is common to save that object to a variable and call several things on it in turn. The grouped object is a thing you hold onto and reuse.
Polars has neither of these. There is no grouped DataFrame that carries the grouping around, and there is nothing to ungroup, because the grouping never outlives the operation that consumes it. You do not store a GroupBy in a variable and come back to it later. You chain straight past it. If your mental model is “group the data, do a few things to it, then ungroup,” set that model aside here. In Polars the grouping exists only in the gap between .group_by and the method on the next line.

title: 5  Grouping and Aggregation
text:
5.5 Aggregation
Looking at individual rows tells us only so much. Most of the time we want a summary: one number that stands in for a whole group. The .agg() method (short for aggregate) is the main tool for this. It lets us say exactly which numbers to compute and which columns to compute them from.

(
    country
    .group_by(c.region)
    .agg(
      hdi_avg = c.hdi.mean()
    )
)


shape: (5, 2)



region
hdi_avg

"Africa"

"Oceania"

"Americas"

"Europe"

Here we compute the average HDI across the countries in each region. The result is a new DataFrame with one row per region. Notice what is in it: the grouping column, region, and the column we asked .agg to build, hdi_avg. Everything else from the original data is gone. This is the defining behavior of aggregation, and we return to it at the end of the chapter.
We can compute several numbers at once by listing more items inside .agg. A useful one to include is pl.len(), which counts the rows in each group.

(
    country
    .group_by(c.region)
    .agg(
        hdi_avg = c.hdi.mean(),
        hdi_median = c.hdi.median(),
        count = pl.len()
    )
)


shape: (5, 4)

Now each row of the result gives the mean HDI, the median HDI, and the number of countries for one region. Reading across a row tells us about that region; reading down a column compares the regions on one measure.
If you run the last two examples yourself, you may notice something unsettling: the order of the rows can change from one run to the next. You did not break anything. When Polars groups data it builds the groups in parallel using a hash table, and the order in which finished groups come out is not fixed. If you want a predictable order, pass maintain_order=True to .group_by, which returns the groups in the order their first row appears in the original data.

(
    country
    .group_by(c.region, maintain_order=True)
    .agg(
        hdi_avg = c.hdi.mean(),
        count = pl.len()
    )
)

There is a modest cost to maintaining order, which is why it is off by default. For a final table you are about to read or print, turning it on is usually the right call. For an intermediate step in a longer pipeline, leave it off.
So far every group has been defined by a single variable, but that is not a requirement. We can pass several columns to .group_by, and a group is then every combination of their values that actually occurs in the data. To show this without introducing a new dataset, we first add a column recording whether a country lies in the Northern Hemisphere, then group by region and by that column together.

(
    country
    .with_columns(north = c.lat >= 0)
    .group_by(c.region, c.north, maintain_order=True)
    .agg(
        hdi_avg = c.hdi.mean(),
        count = pl.len()
    )
)
