The result has one row for each region-and-hemisphere combination that exists in the data. A region whose countries all sit north of the equator produces a single row; a region split across the equator produces two.
Just as the grouping can be richer than a single column, so can the thing we aggregate. It does not have to be a bare column; it can be an expression, and this is where aggregation starts to earn its keep. Suppose we want to count, within each region, how many countries have a population above one hundred million. The comparison c.pop > 100 produces a column of true and false values (population in this dataset is measured in millions), and calling .sum() on a boolean column counts the true entries.

(
    country
    .group_by(c.region, maintain_order=True)
    .agg(
        n_large = (c.pop > 100).sum()
    )
)

We never had to create a “large country” column in the data. The expression was built, evaluated per group, and reduced to a count, all inside .agg. Any expression you could write in .with_columns can be aggregated this way, which means the set of questions you can ask is much larger than the list of built-in summary functions.

title: 5  Grouping and Aggregation
text:
5.6 Counting
Counting rows by group is probably the single most common thing you will do with grouped data, so it pays to be precise about it. Polars gives you a few ways to count, and they answer slightly different questions.
The clearest way to count rows is pl.len(). It returns the number of rows in each group and does not depend on any particular column, which is exactly what you want when the question is simply “how many countries are in this region.”
There is also a .count() method that you call on a specific column. It counts the non-missing values in that column, not the rows. When a column has no missing data the two give the same answer, but when it does, .count() is smaller. The example below shows the difference: n is the number of countries in each region, while n_hdi is the number of those countries that actually have an HDI value recorded.

(
    country
    .group_by(c.region, maintain_order=True)
    .agg(
        n = pl.len(),
        n_hdi = c.hdi.count()
    )
)


shape: (5, 3)

Wherever n_hdi is smaller than n, some countries in that region are missing an HDI. That gap is often something you want to know before you trust a group average.
Finally, .n_unique() counts distinct non-missing values in a column. It answers “how many different values appear,” which is a different question from “how many rows.” Counting the distinct regions in the whole dataset, for instance, uses the same method without any grouping:

(
    country
    .select(n_regions = c.region.n_unique())
)


shape: (1, 1)



n_regions


u32




5






For most work pl.len() is what you reach for. Use .count() when you specifically care about non-missing values, and .n_unique() when you care about distinct ones.

title: 5  Grouping and Aggregation
text:
5.7 Aggregating the Whole Frame
Sometimes there are no groups. You want one summary of the entire dataset: the mean population across all countries, the total, the number of rows. It is tempting to call .agg without a .group_by in front of it, but Polars does not allow that. Unlike R and pandas, .agg here always belongs to a grouping.
The solution is .select, using exactly the same aggregation syntax you would put inside .agg. We met .select earlier as a way to choose columns, but when the expressions inside it are aggregations, it collapses the whole frame to a single row.

(
    country
    .select(
        pop_total = c.pop.sum(),
        hdi_avg = c.hdi.mean(),
        n = pl.len()
    )
)


shape: (1, 3)



pop_total
hdi_avg
n


f64
f64
u32




7802.082
0.757044
135






The result is a one-row DataFrame with a column for each summary we asked for. Using the column selector to aggregate looks odd the first time, but it fits how Polars thinks: .select builds new columns from expressions, and an aggregating expression happens to build a column with one value in it. You can read a whole-frame summary as a group-by with a single group covering everything.

title: 5  Grouping and Aggregation
text:
5.8 Aggregation as Row Reduction
It helps to name the pattern that runs through this chapter, because the next one is built around pushing back on it. Every aggregation reduces: it takes the many rows of a group and returns exactly one row for that group. After you compute the average HDI per region, the individual countries are no longer in the result. Their values went into the average and then the rows themselves were discarded. That is the whole point when the answer you want is one number per group.
But it raises a natural question. What if you want a group-level number and you also want to keep the rows? Say you want each country labeled with its share of its own region’s total population. The number is computed per group, but you want it attached to every country, not collapsed down to one row per region. Aggregation cannot do this, because reducing the rows is the one thing it always does. The next chapter introduces the tool for computing a value per group while leaving every original row in place.

title: 5  Grouping and Aggregation
text:
5.9 Aggregation Functions
Polars offers a range of functions to use inside .agg (and inside .select for whole-frame summaries). These cover most of what comes up in practice:

.mean(), .median(), .min(), .max(), .sum(), .quantile(<f64>)
.first(), .last(), .any(), .all()
.n_unique(), .count(), .len()
pl.corr(..., ...) for the correlation between two columns

Each of the methods in the first three groups is called on a column, as in c.hdi.mean(). Remember that you can call them on an expression just as easily as on a bare column, which extends this short list a long way.

title: 6  Window Functions
text:
6.1 Setup
This chapter uses two datasets: the country data and the cellphone data introduced in Chapter 4, which records the number of cellphone subscriptions in each country over several decades. We load plotnine as well, since the chapter ends with a plot.
import polars as pl
from plotnine import ggplot, aes
from polars import col as c

import funs

country = pl.read_csv("data/countries.csv")
cellphone = pl.read_csv("data/countries_cellphone.csv")

title: 6  Window Functions
text:
6.2 Introduction
The previous chapter ended with a limitation. Aggregation answers questions about groups by collapsing each group to a single row: one mean per region, one count per subregion. The individual rows are gone from the result, and with them any chance of comparing a row to its group. This chapter introduces window functions, which compute the same group-level quantities but attach the answers to the original rows instead of replacing them. The contrast is the organizing idea of the chapter, and it is worth stating plainly: aggregation reduces rows, window functions preserve them.
The tools here are also where row order starts to matter. Alongside grouped computations, we cover functions that look up and down a column, such as shifting values by a row, accumulating running totals, and averaging over a sliding window. These are the natural tools for data with a time dimension, and the cellphone data gives us one.

title: 6  Window Functions
text:
6.3 The Problem
Suppose we want each country’s share of its region’s population. This is a perfectly ordinary question, and it needs two ingredients on every row: the country’s own population, which is already there, and the region’s total, which is not. With the tools of Chapter 5 we can compute the regional totals easily enough:

(
    country
    .group_by(c.region)
    .agg(pop_total = c.pop.sum())
)


shape: (5, 2)



region
pop_total


str
f64




"Europe"
733.183


"Asia"
4732.458


"Africa"
1330.091


"Oceania"
32.226


"Americas"
974.124






But now we are stuck. The totals live in a small summary table with one row per region, and the populations live in the original table with one row per country. Dividing one by the other requires lining the two tables up, which is a join, a technique we will not meet until Chapter 8. Even once we have joins, using one here means building a second table and matching it back, all to move seven numbers into place.
What we actually want is to skip the detour: compute the regional total and hand it directly to every country in that region, without the summary table ever existing on its own. That is exactly what a window function does.

title: 6  Window Functions
text:
6.4 The over Method
The .over() method turns an aggregation into a window function. Written after an aggregation such as .sum(), it says: compute this separately within each group, then write the group’s answer onto every row of that group. The population-share problem becomes a single with_columns:

(
    country
    .with_columns(
        pop_share = c.pop / c.pop.sum().over(c.region) * 100
    )
    .sort(c.pop_share, descending=True)
)

Read the new expression from the inside out. c.pop.sum() is a description of a sum, and .over(c.region) scopes that sum to each region. The result has the same 135 rows as country; no rows were lost, and the regional total appears, repeated, on every row of its region. The division then happens row by row, exactly as in Chapter 2. Every country in the output now carries its share, and the sort brings the regionally dominant countries to the top.






NoteWhere to put .over()





You may encounter code that writes the window the other way around, as c.pop.over(c.region).sum(), with the aggregation last. Earlier versions of this book used that form, and it still runs in many cases. Current Polars documents and recommends the order used here, aggregation first and .over() second, which also reads more naturally: “the sum of pop, within each region.” We use that order throughout.



To make the contrast with aggregation concrete, here is the same quantity, the average HDI of each region, computed both ways. First with .agg(), which returns one row per region:

    country
    .group_by(c.region)

title: 6  Window Functions
text:
6.5 Shift and Ordered Windows
The windows so far ignore the order of rows within a group; a sum is a sum however the rows are arranged. A second family of functions depends on order directly. The simplest is .shift(<i64>), which moves a column’s values down by the given number of rows, so that each row sees the value from <i64> rows above it. Negative values shift upward instead.
The example below, carried over from an earlier chapter’s brief preview of this idea, sorts countries from largest to smallest and asks how much larger each country is than the one just below it:

(
    country
    .sort(c.pop, descending=True)
    .with_columns(
        perc_larger = c.pop / c.pop.shift(-1) * 100
    )
)

Because the meaning of “the row above” depends entirely on the sort order, a .shift() should almost always sit directly after the .sort() that gives it that meaning.
Shifting becomes far more useful on time series, and this is where the cellphone data comes in. Each row records one country in one year, so the year-over-year change in subscriptions is each row’s value minus the value one row earlier, once the rows are sorted by year. But there is a trap. If we sort the whole table and shift, the first year of each country would subtract the last year of the previous country, producing one nonsense value at every seam. The shift needs to restart at each country boundary, and .over() is how we say so:

(
    cellphone
    .sort(c.iso, c.year)
    .with_columns(
        change = c.cell - c.cell.shift(1).over(c.iso)
    )
)

Within each country, every year now shows its gain over the previous year. The first year of each country has no previous year, so its change is null, and the null propagates through the subtraction just as Chapter 2 described. This combination, an ordered function scoped by .over(), is the workhorse pattern of the chapter, and it is worth pausing to read the expression again until both parts feel separate: shift(1) says look one row up, and .over(c.iso) says but never across a country boundary.
Polars offers a shortcut, .diff(), that computes the difference with the shifted value in one step, but writing out the subtraction makes the mechanics visible, so we will keep doing that for now.
The neighbor-based filling methods promised in Chapter 2 also live in this family. .forward_fill() replaces each missing value with the closest valid value above it, and .backward_fill() with the closest below. On the cellphone data, a country with a gap year can borrow its most recent known count:

(
    cellphone
    .sort(c.iso, c.year)
    .with_columns(
        cell = c.cell.forward_fill().over(c.iso)
    )
)

The same trap applies as with .shift(): without the .over(), a country missing its early years would silently inherit counts from whatever country happens to sort just before it.

title: 6  Window Functions
text:
6.6 More Ordered Tools
Two more ordered tools round out the family. The .rank() method assigns each value its position within the column, and takes a descending argument so that rank 1 can mean the largest value rather than the smallest. Scoped with .over(), it ranks within groups. The code below finds each country’s HDI rank within its region:

(
    country
    .with_columns(
        hdi_rank = c.hdi.rank(descending=True).over(c.region)
    )
    .select(c.iso, c.region, c.hdi, c.hdi_rank)
    .sort(c.region, c.hdi_rank)
)

Unlike .shift(), the .rank() method does not care how the rows are sorted; it computes positions from the values themselves. The final .sort() above only affects how the result is displayed.
The .cum_sum() method computes a running total, where each row shows the sum of every value up to and including itself. Here order matters again. One revealing use is the cumulative share of world population after sorting countries from largest to smallest:

(
    country
    .sort(c.pop, descending=True)
    .select(
        c.iso,
        c.pop,
        cum_share = c.pop.cum_sum() / c.pop.sum() * 100
    )
)

Note the two aggregations playing different roles in one expression: .cum_sum() is a running total that changes on every row, while .sum() is the plain fixed total from Chapter 5. Like the other functions in this section, .cum_sum() accepts .over() when the running total should restart within each group.

title: 6  Window Functions
text:
6.7 Rolling Windows
This short section can be skipped on a first reading; nothing later in the book depends on it.
A rolling window computes a statistic over a fixed span of nearby rows, most commonly a mean, so that each row is replaced by the average of itself and its recent neighbors. The effect is to smooth out short-term fluctuations so that a longer trend is easier to see. The .rolling_mean() method takes the span as window_size:

(
    cellphone
    .filter(c.iso == "USA")
    .sort(c.year)
    .with_columns(
        cell_smooth = c.cell.rolling_mean(window_size=5)
    )
)

Each row of cell_smooth is the average of that year and the four before it, which is why the first four rows are null: they do not yet have a full window behind them. As with every ordered function in this chapter, the sort determines what “nearby” means, and adding .over(c.iso) would keep the window from sliding across country boundaries if we computed this for the full table.

title: 6  Window Functions
text:
6.8 Aggregation Versus Over
With both tools in hand, the choice between them comes down to one question: what should the result look like? If the answer is a table of groups, one row each, use group_by and agg. If the answer is the original table with group information written onto it, or filtered by it, use over. Aggregation reduces rows, window functions preserve them, and neither is a substitute for the other. In practice the two often appear in the same analysis: an agg to report regional summaries, an over to flag which countries drive them.
Readers coming from dplyr have seen the second pattern before under a different guise. A grouped tibble passed to mutate() or filter() behaves like our over expressions, computing within groups while keeping the rows. Polars simply attaches the grouping to the expression rather than to the table. The Pandas equivalent is groupby() followed by .transform(), which many Pandas users meet late or not at all; if that method never quite made sense, over is the same idea with the pieces laid out in the open.
We close with a plot, partly as a preview of Chapter 7 and partly because it shows a window that runs in the other direction. Every window so far grouped by country and looked across years. The plot below does the reverse: within each year, it computes each country’s share of the group’s combined subscriptions, then draws how those shares shift over time.

(
    cellphone
    .filter(c.iso.is_in(["BRA", "CHN", "IND", "NGA", "USA"]))
    .with_columns(
        share = c.cell / c.cell.sum().over(c.year) * 100
    )
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.year, c.share, color="iso"))
    .geom_line()
)








The raw subscription counts for these five countries all rise steeply, which makes the lines hard to compare directly; the shares put the countries on a common scale, so relative gains and losses are visible at a glance. The figure could not have been drawn from an aggregated table, because the plot needs one point per country per year, and aggregation would have collapsed exactly the rows the lines are made of. Plots that instead need the collapsed table, such as bar charts of group counts, are the opening topic of Chapter 7.

title: 6  Window Functions
text:
6.9 Extensions
Polars provides a larger set of window and ordered functions than the ones covered here, including .cum_max(), .cum_count(), .rank() with alternative tie-breaking methods, and time-aware rolling operations such as .rolling_mean_by(), which define the window by a date span rather than a row count. All are documented in the expressions section of the Polars user guide. Readers who go on to work with databases will meet this chapter again: window functions originate in SQL, where the same aggregate-versus-window distinction appears as GROUP BY versus OVER, and the Polars method takes its name from there.

title: 7  Visualizing Data II
text:
7.1 Setup
Load all of the modules and datasets needed for the chapter.
import polars as pl
from plotnine import ggplot, aes
from polars import col as c

import funs

country = pl.read_csv("data/countries.csv")
cellphone = pl.read_csv("data/countries_cellphone.csv")

title: 7  Visualizing Data II
text:
7.2 Introduction
In Chapter 4 we built visualizations one row at a time. Each point, each text label, and each line segment came directly from a single record in the data. In Chapter 5 we learned to do something quite different: group the rows of a table and compute a summary for each group with group_by and agg. This chapter joins those two ideas together.
That join is the whole point of the geometries we start with here. A bar chart of counts, a boxplot for each region, a histogram: none of these draw one shape per row. Each one first runs a grouped computation over the data and then draws the result. Once you see that a statistical geometry is a group_by and agg in disguise, the rest of this chapter is mostly about control and polish: choosing the scales that map data to color and position, splitting a plot into small panels, and labeling everything so the result is ready to show to someone else.

title: 7  Visualizing Data II
text:
7.3 Statistical Geometries
Every geometry we have used so far draws one object per row. A point geometry places one point per country; a text geometry writes one label per country; a line geometry connects one row to the next. The geometries in the next few sections work differently. Before drawing anything, they run a computation over groups of rows, and the objects in the finished plot correspond to that computation rather than to individual records.
The clearest way to see this is to do the computation ourselves first. Suppose we want to know how many countries fall into each subregion. In Chapter 5 we would answer that by grouping and counting.

(
    country
    .group_by(c.subregion)
    .agg(pl.len().alias("n"))
    .sort(c.n)
)


shape: (19, 2)



subregion
n


str
u32




"Caribbean"
1


"Australia and New Zealand"
2


"Northern America"
2


"Southern Africa"
3


"Central Asia"
4


…
…


"Northern Europe"
10


"Eastern Africa"
11


"Southern Europe"
12


"Western Africa"
14


"Western Asia"
15






The result is a small table: one row per subregion, with a count next to each. Now look at what happens when we hand the raw country table to geom_bar and map a single aesthetic to subregion. We change the default colors so the bars are easier to read and flip the coordinates so the labels do not overlap.

(
    country
    .pipe(ggplot, aes("reorder(subregion, subregion, len)"))
    .geom_bar(fill="white", color="black")
    .coord_flip()
)








The height of each bar is exactly the count we computed by hand a moment ago. geom_bar took a categorical aesthetic, grouped the rows by its value, counted the rows in each group, and drew each count as a bar. It did the group_by and agg for us and then rendered the answer. That is what “statistics-based geometry” means for the rest of this chapter.
Letting geom_bar count for us is convenient, but in practice the number you want to plot is often already sitting in a frame. Maybe you computed it with group_by and agg, or it arrived from a database or a colleague already summarized. For that case there is geom_col, which does no counting of its own. You give it an x and a y, and it draws each y value as the height of a bar.
We can reproduce the plot above by aggregating first and piping the result straight into ggplot. The chain reads as one continuous thought: group the rows, summarize each group, then plot.

(
    country
    .group_by("subregion")
    .agg(pl.len().alias("n"))
    .pipe(ggplot, aes("reorder(subregion, n)", c.n))
    .geom_col(fill="white", color="black")
    .coord_flip()
)








The bars are the same, but the labor is split differently. Here we counted the countries ourselves and geom_col only drew the numbers. This pattern, aggregate and then pipe into a column geometry, is the one you will use most often once your data grows past toy examples, because real summaries usually involve more than a plain count: an average price per category, a total revenue per month, a median score per group. Compute the summary you want with the tools from Chapter 5, then plot it with geom_col.
The two geometries look nearly identical on the page and are a reliable source of confusion, so it is worth stating the difference plainly: it is a question of who does the counting. geom_bar takes a single categorical aesthetic and counts the rows in each category for you, so the height of each bar is a tally it computes internally. geom_col takes both an x and a y and draws each y value as given, so you supply the height. Reach for geom_bar when you want the raw count of how many rows fall into each category and you would rather not compute it yourself, and reach for geom_col when the number you want to show is already in the frame. If you ever find yourself computing a count with group_by only to feed it to geom_bar, you wanted geom_col.

title: 7  Visualizing Data II
text:
7.4 Ordering Categories
We have now used reorder several times without pinning down what it does, so it is worth being precise. Written inside an aesthetic as reorder(category, by, func), it takes the categorical column category, computes one summary of by within each category using func, and orders the categories by that summary.
In the population bar chart back in Chapter 4, reorder(full_name, pop) ordered the countries by their population using the default summary. In the count plots above, reorder(subregion, subregion, len) grouped the rows by subregion, measured each group with len, and put the subregions in order of that count. The mechanism is the same grouped computation that sits behind every statistical geometry. The only difference is that here the summary is used to fix an order rather than to draw a shape.
A companion helper, factor, is also written inside the quotes passed to a plotnine function. It forces a column to be treated as categorical even when it is stored as a number. Years are the usual case. A column holding values like 2000, 2005, and 2010 is stored as integers, so plotnine will spread them along a continuous axis with a smooth range of grid lines. Wrapping the aesthetic as factor(year) tells plotnine to treat each distinct year as its own category, which is what you want when the years label separate bars or boxes instead of marking positions on a continuous scale.

title: 7  Visualizing Data II
text:
7.5 Distributions
Counting works for categories. For a numeric column we first have to decide what a “group” even is, because no two countries share the exact same HDI. A histogram answers this by cutting the number line into equal intervals, called bins, and counting how many values fall into each one. It is still a grouped count; the groups are just intervals rather than named categories. We create one with geom_histogram. The output is easiest to read if we set binwidth and boundary, which control the width of each bin and where the bins start relative to zero.

(
    country
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.hdi))
    .geom_histogram(fill="white", color="black", binwidth=0.1, boundary=0)
)














NoteInterpreting a histogram





If interpreting a histogram feels difficult, walk through one bar. Take the example above and look at the bar spanning 0.4 to 0.5 on the x-axis. Its height on the y-axis is about 11, which means 11 countries have an HDI between 0.4 and 0.5. In the same way, roughly 26 countries fall between 0.8 and 0.9, and about 33 between 0.9 and 1.0.



We can instead set bins to a whole number to fix the total count of bins. That is quicker when we do not yet know the scale of the data, though the resulting counts are harder to read off directly.
A density plot is an alternative that models the distribution rather than tallying it into buckets. The adjust option, which defaults to 1, controls how smooth the curve is. Larger values give a smoother curve and smaller values follow the data more closely, so it is worth trying a few to find the one that shows the pattern you care about.

(
    country
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.hdi))
    .geom_density(fill="white", color="black", adjust=0.8)
)














NoteInterpreting a density plot





The total area under a density curve is 1. The area under the curve between two points gives the proportion of observations that fall between those values. With a larger dataset the density settles into the same shape as the histogram, differing only in scale and in being somewhat smoother.



Histograms and density plots describe one numeric column on its own. A boxplot combines that idea with the counting of categories we started from: it summarizes a numeric variable separately for each level of a categorical one. To see the grouped computation underneath it, we can compute the pieces ourselves. A box is built from three numbers per group, the 25th, 50th, and 75th percentiles of the values in that group.

(
    country
    .group_by(c.region)
    .agg(
        q25=c.hdi.quantile(0.25),
        q50=c.hdi.quantile(0.50),
        q75=c.hdi.quantile(0.75),
    )
    .sort(c.q50)
)


shape: (5, 4)



region
q25
q50
q75


str
f64
f64
f64




"Africa"
0.51
0.571
0.649


"Asia"
0.691
0.766
0.852


"Americas"
0.733
0.786
0.839


"Europe"
0.862
0.915
0.948


"Oceania"
0.938
0.958
0.958






Each row of this table is a box waiting to be drawn: the box spans the 25th to the 75th percentile, and a line marks the median at the 50th. geom_boxplot computes these same quantiles per group and adds two more elements, a pair of lines called whiskers that reach out to show where most of the remaining values lie, and individual points for outliers beyond them. As with the bar charts, reorder and coord_flip make the result easier to read.

(
    country
    .pipe(ggplot, aes("reorder(region, hdi)", c.hdi))
    .geom_boxplot()
    .coord_flip()
)

title: 7  Visualizing Data II
text:
7.6 Fitting a Model
The last statistical geometry summarizes differently. Rather than grouping rows, geom_smooth fits a model to the relationship between two numeric variables and draws the fitted curve. With the option method="lm" it fits a straight line of best fit. By default it also draws a confidence band around that line, which we turn off below by setting se=False.

(
    country
    .filter(c.region == "Europe")
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.hdi, c.happy))
    .geom_point()
    .geom_smooth(method="lm", se=False)
)








Calling geom_smooth() without a method produces a more flexible, non-linear curve, which is useful when the relationship is clearly not a straight line.
All of the statistical geometries we have seen, from geom_bar through geom_smooth, accept a group or color aesthetic that carries out the summary separately for each value of that aesthetic. This gets crowded quickly and is only advisable with a small number of groups, at most four or five.

title: 7  Visualizing Data II
text:
7.7 Scales
Python makes many choices for us automatically when it builds a plot. When we mapped the color of the points to a variable, Python decided which specific colors to use. It also chose how large to make the axes, where to place tick marks, and where to draw grid lines. Leaving these details to Python is convenient because it lets us stay focused on the data. Sometimes, though, when we are preparing a plot for others or the defaults are hard to read, we want to set them ourselves. That is what scales are for.
Each aesthetic in the grammar of graphics has a scale attached to it. A scale describes how the plot turns an aesthetic into something you can actually see. The scale for the x aesthetic sets the smallest and largest values on the horizontal axis and decides how to label it. A color scale sets which color goes with each category and how to lay out the legend. To change a default scale, we add a scale function to the plot. Its position relative to the geometries does not matter, and by convention we group scales after the geometries.
A popular alternative to the default color palette is scale_color_cmap_d(). It builds a set of colors that are friendly to color-blind readers, print well in black and white, and hold up on a bad projector. After mapping a geometry’s color to a column, add the scale as another line.

(
    country
    .filter(c.region == "Europe")
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.hdi, c.happy))
    .geom_point(aes(color=c.subregion))
    .scale_color_cmap_d()
)








The colors now run from dark purple to bright yellow instead of the default rainbow. As with the bar charts, the categories are assigned colors in alphabetical order; changing that order means changing the data before it reaches the plot, using the grouping and ordering tools from Chapter 5. The _d at the end of the function marks it as discrete, meaning it maps colors to a categorical variable. A matching function, scale_color_cmap(), produces a similar palette when the color varies with a numeric variable.
Many other scales exist. For example, scale_size_area makes point sizes proportional to a column, and scale_x_log10() and scale_y_log10() put an axis on a logarithmic scale, which helps with heavily skewed data. We will use these as they come up.
The default scale for the x-axis is scale_x_continuous, with scale_y_continuous playing the same role for the y-axis. Adding either one on its own changes nothing, but both take optional arguments that adjust how the plot is drawn. Setting breaks controls where the labels and grid lines fall along the axis; we usually set it with a helper such as breaks_width, which places a break at a fixed spacing. Setting limits to a pair of numbers fixes the start and end of the plotted range, and we can pass None for one end to leave that side to the default. The code below draws the same data as our original scatter plot, but with grid lines and labels every 0.05 of HDI along the horizontal axis and a vertical axis pinned to start at zero.

(
    country
    .filter(c.region == "Europe")
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.hdi, c.happy))
    .geom_point()
    .scale_x_continuous(breaks=breaks_width(0.05))
    .scale_y_continuous(limits=(0, None))
)

Finally, two scale types are useful for working with colors directly. Sometimes a column already holds the color for each observation, in which case scale_color_identity tells the plot to use those colors as given. Another option is scale_color_manual, which lets you assign an exact color to each category. Below is the syntax for setting a color per subregion in the countries data.

(
    country
    .filter(c.region == "Europe")
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.hdi, c.happy))
    .geom_point(aes(color=c.subregion))
    .scale_color_manual(values={
        "Eastern Europe": "lightblue",
        "Northern Europe": "navy",
        "Southern Europe": "peachpuff",
        "Western Europe": "wheat"
    })
)

Manual colors make sense when the groups have well-known colors of their own. Plotting data about political parties, for instance, reads better in each party’s traditional color. The plotnine documentation lists many more scales for customizing a plot.

title: 7  Visualizing Data II
text:
7.8 Facets
Facets are another way to show differences across the levels of a categorical variable. Instead of packing every group into one plot, they lay out a grid of small panels, splitting the data by one or two variables. We add them with facet_wrap() and the name of the grouping variable. It is common, as below, to also map color to the grouping variable and hide the legend, so the color reinforces which panel holds which group.

(
    country
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.hdi, c.happy))
    .geom_point(aes(color=c.region), show_legend=False)
    .facet_wrap("region")
)








The space option can be set to fixed (the default), free_x, free_y, or free to decide whether the panels share the same scales. A related function, facet_grid, takes two variables and arranges the panels across both rows and columns.
Facets can make certain relationships far easier to read and give your output a polished, professional look. Use them with some restraint, though. People new to data science tend to overuse them badly. They work best when you have only a few categories and have already used the other tools in this chapter and the last to bring out the patterns in the data.

title: 7  Visualizing Data II
text:
7.9 Labels and Themes
We have built and adjusted a good number of plots by now without ever touching the axis labels. During exploratory work that is usually the right call. The default labels take no effort, which makes it painless to swap variables and settings without stopping to relabel anything. They also use the exact column names from your data, which are the names you need when you refer to those variables in later plots, models, and data manipulation. Once you are presenting results to someone else, though, the axes and legends deserve fuller descriptions, and the grammar of graphics lets us add them directly.
To change the labels, we add a labs layer. Inside it, we assign text to the aesthetics we want to describe; anything we leave out keeps its default. The x and y labels appear beside the plot, while labels for aesthetics like size and color appear in the legend. We can also give labs a title, a subtitle, and a caption to describe the plot as a whole.

(
    country
    .filter(c.region == "Europe")
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(c.hdi, c.happy))
    .geom_point(aes(color=c.subregion))
    .labs(
        x="Human Development Index (HDI)",
        y="Happiness Score",
        color="Subregion",
        title="Relationship between HDI and Happiness",
        subtitle="Europe",
        caption="Data from the Gapminder"
    )
)








The other way to prepare a plot for distribution is to change its theme. Themes govern the look of the non-data elements: axis labels, ticks, grid lines, and the background. We have been using theme_minimal() throughout, which is a good fit for exploration because it strips away clutter while keeping the grid lines that help us read values. For external publication we often prefer something even sparer, in the spirit of Edward Tufte’s work on data visualization, which aims to spend as little ink as possible on anything other than the data itself [1]. We select a theme by adding its function to the plot, or by setting it once with theme_set so that every later plot picks it up.

theme_set(theme_minimal())

A minimal theme can feel too bare when you are first poking at a dataset, but it is an excellent choice for the final version. There are many resources online for tuning a theme further to suit your needs.

title: 7  Visualizing Data II
text:
7.10 Best Practices
Across this chapter and the last we have covered how to make a wide range of graphics. It helps to close with a few guiding principles about which graphic to reach for. These are defaults, not laws, and specific tasks will sometimes call for breaking them.

Use bar plots, histograms, and density plots to understand the distribution of a single variable.
Use scatter plots and boxplots to understand the relationship between two variables.
Use scatter plots with text labels to understand individual data points, either on their own or against a larger set.
Only use a variable color scale after you have used the x and y aesthetics as fully as you can.
Treat size and color for continuous variables as an extra layer of information, never the primary one. Do not use size for categorical variables.
Use color and shape only with a small set of categories. The cutoff depends on the dataset and the task, but eight is a reasonable rule of thumb.
Reserve line plots for a single quantity measured over time, space, or another continuous variable.

The best way to learn which plot fits which task, and when to break these rules, is to see many examples. We will build up that library throughout the rest of the book.

title: 7  Visualizing Data II
text:
References




[1] Tufte, E (1987 ). The Visual Display of Quantitative Information. Graphics Press

title: 8  Combining Tables
text:
8.1 Setup
Load all of the modules and datasets needed for the chapter.
import polars as pl
from plotnine import ggplot, aes
from polars import col as c

import funs

country = pl.read_csv("data/countries.csv")

title: 8  Combining Tables
text:
8.2 Introduction
In Chapter 3, we used data operations to modify a single dataset in Python: filtering rows, selecting columns, adding new columns, sorting, and grouping-and-summarizing. All of that worked with one table at a time. Real projects rarely stop there. A dataset about customers usually needs to be linked to a dataset about products; a dataset about countries needs to be linked to a dataset about their borders. This chapter is about combining tables of data using joins, the primary tool for connecting information stored in separate places.

title: 8  Combining Tables
text:
8.3 Primary and Foreign Keys
Primary keys are a core concept we need to understand to combine two datasets in Python. A primary key consists of one or more columns that can uniquely and minimally identify a row of data. For example, in our country dataset the column iso is a primary key. The column full_name is also a primary key. In database theory a distinction is made between candidate keys (any set of one or more columns that could serve as a primary key) and the specific primary key chosen for a table. This distinction is less applicable to data science work, where we will use the term primary key to refer to any possible way of identifying a row in our dataset.
A foreign key is a primary key from one table that appears in another table. For example, we might have a dataset of customers, with one column indicating the iso of the country in which they live. We join multiple tables by associating the primary key in one table with the matching foreign key in another. Notice that the difference between primary and foreign is the table in which the key appears, not the information itself. The value iso is both a primary key in the countries dataset and a foreign key in the customers dataset. The connection between these two tables is called a relation. This is where the term relational database comes from.

title: 8  Combining Tables
text:
8.4 Joining by Relation
We will start with an abstract example before turning to a specific example using actual DataFrames in Python. Assume we have two tables, table_one and table_two. The column key_col appears in both tables: it is a primary key in table_two and a foreign key in table_one. This means it has a single unique row associated with it in table_two but possibly many instances in table_one.
To join the information in these two tables, we use the .join() method of the table containing the foreign key. The arguments include the second dataset and the name of the key used to combine the tables.

(
    table_one
    .join(table_two, on=c.key_col)
)

Assuming our data are correctly organized, the output will be a dataset with the same number of rows as table_one, where the first set of columns will also match table_one. After the original columns, there will be a version of all the columns from table_two that correspond to the matching values of our key.
Often some of the values in table_one will not have a matching value in table_two. In that case, the default behavior is to remove rows without matches; this is called an inner join. Alternatively, you can fill missing columns with null values, which results in a left join. We can indicate this to Python as follows:

(
    table_one
    .join(table_two, on=c.key_col, how="left")
)







NoteLeft and right tables





The terminology of “left” and “right” tables comes from thinking about how the join would look when written on a single line. Consider the code table_one.join(table_two); the first table is to the left of the join function and the second table is to the right of the join function. As a rule of thumb, we try to place the table with the foreign key on the left and the table with the primary key on the right. The reason for this is that the output should match the number of observations in the table with the foreign key, so it typically makes it easier to reason about treating it as a method of the foreign-key table. Note that usually the table with the foreign key will be much larger than the table with the primary key.









NoteEqui-joins: inner, left, right, full, semi, anti, cross





There are six different options that we can assign to the how parameter in the table join method. The default, inner, returns only rows that match in both tables. Left joins return all rows from the left DataFrame; right joins return all rows from the right DataFrame. Full joins (rarely used) return all rows from both tables. A semi join returns only rows from the left table that have a match in the right table; no additional columns are added. An anti join returns only rows in the left table that do not match any rows in the right table. These six options are called equi-joins because they work by finding where the key(s) in one table are equal to the key(s) in another table. We’ll come back to semi- and anti-joins below, since they are useful enough to deserve their own worked example.
A cross join returns all combinations of rows from the two tables, with no key required. It has several interesting applications, which we will see in later chapters.



There are several other modifications that we can apply to the basic syntax. For example, if our key has multiple columns, we can pass a list to on instead of a single value. Here is the code to join on two matching columns from the two DataFrames.

(
    table_one
    .join(table_two, on=[c.key_col1, c.key_col2])
)

Or, if the key has different names in the two tables, we can describe them with left_on (the first table) and right_on (the second table). These can be individual values or lists, where each element in one list corresponds to the element in the other.

(
    table_one
    .join(table_two, left_on=c.key_col1, right_on=c.key_col1)
)

Note that Python will let you join on any set of columns that match between the two tables, whether or not either column is actually a primary key. When the join column is not unique in the right-hand table, the result can be surprising, and possibly very large — we’ll see exactly what that looks like, and how to catch it before it happens, in Section 8.7 below. Avoid joins that do not follow the pattern here (foreign key in the left table and a primary key in the right table).

title: 8  Combining Tables
text:
8.5 Join Examples
The code outline in the previous section should make more sense once we see examples using actual datasets. To do this, we need multiple datasets that we can join together. We will create a smaller version of the country dataset that contains only two columns, which makes it easier to view the results without scrolling. We also load a dataset of country borders, where each row shows the relationship between a country and one of its neighbors.

c_sml = country.select(c.iso, c.gini)
border = pl.read_csv("data/countries_borders.csv")
border

As noted above, we can add the option how= with the values inner (default), left, right, or full to indicate what should happen when keys appear only in one table. Left joins retain all keys from the first (left) table, right joins retain all keys from the second (right) table, and full joins retain all keys from both tables. When there is no matching value in the other table, null values are filled in. The default, inner, is often a good choice to avoid missing values.

title: 8  Combining Tables
text:
8.6 Filtering Joins: Semi and Anti
Not every join is meant to add columns. Sometimes we just want to know which rows in one table have — or don’t have — a match in another table, without pulling in any of the second table’s columns. These are called filtering joins, because they act like the .filter() method: they select a subset of rows rather than adding new information. There are two variants. A semi-join keeps only the rows in the left table that have a match in the right table. An anti-join keeps only the rows in the left table that do not have a match. When there is a single key column, semi- and anti-joins can be replaced by a call to .is_in(); their real value shows up once there are multiple key columns and we want to know whether a specific combination matches, which .is_in() cannot express.
Filtering joins are also a quick way to check data quality. Above, we saw that joining border to c_sml on iso dropped the DataFrame from 829 rows down to 641, because c_sml only contains countries with full economic data. But is that the whole explanation? We can check by comparing border against the full country table (not the trimmed-down c_sml) using an anti-join, which will show us every row in border whose iso value has no match in country at all.

(
    border
    .join(country, on=c.iso, how="anti")
)


shape: (188, 2)



iso
iso_neighbor

Any rows that show up here are not explained by missing economic data — they are iso codes in the border dataset that don’t correspond to any row in the country table at all. In a real project, this is exactly the kind of thing you’d want to chase down: is border including dependent territories or disputed regions that country deliberately excludes, or is there a typo in one of the codes? A semi-join answers the mirror-image question. border.join(country, on=c.iso, how="semi") would keep only the rows of border whose iso value does have a match, without adding any of country’s columns — useful when you want to filter one table down to “things that exist elsewhere” and nothing more.

title: 8  Combining Tables
text:
8.7 Diagnosing Bad Joins
We said above that joining on a column that isn’t a primary key in the right table can produce surprising, and possibly very large, results. Let’s see exactly what that looks like, because this is the single join bug you are most likely to write yourself.
Suppose c_sml had gotten corrupted somewhere upstream — maybe two data sources were merged carelessly — and ended up with two rows for Afghanistan instead of one. We can simulate that here by adding a duplicate row.

bad_c_sml = pl.concat([c_sml, c_sml.filter(c.iso == "AFG")])
bad_c_sml.filter(c.iso == "AFG")

    border
    .join(bad_c_sml, on=c.iso)
)

Recall that the first five rows of border all correspond to Afghanistan. Each one of those rows now matches two rows in bad_c_sml instead of one, so the join produces five extra rows — 646 instead of 641 — all of them duplicate copies of Afghanistan’s borders. Nothing in the code raised an error. Polars did exactly what we asked: it matched every row in the left table against every matching row in the right table, however many there were. If this had been a real dataset and we hadn’t been watching the row count carefully, we might not have noticed the duplication at all, especially in a larger table where the extra rows blend in.
This is why it pays to check the key column before joining, rather than after. Back in Chapter 5 we used n_unique() alongside len() to check whether a column had repeated values; the same check tells us immediately whether a table is safe to use as the right-hand side of a join.

c_sml.select(c.iso).n_unique() == len(c_sml)

True



bad_c_sml.select(c.iso).n_unique() == len(bad_c_sml)

False


The first check passes: iso is a genuine primary key in c_sml, one row per country. The second fails, which tells us — before we’ve joined anything — that bad_c_sml will multiply rows if we use it as the right table. Get in the habit of running this check on the right-hand table any time you’re not certain the join key is unique. It takes one line and it catches the bug at its source instead of downstream, where a silently inflated row count can quietly throw off every summary statistic you compute afterward.

title: 8  Combining Tables
text:
8.8 Conditional Joins
In this section, we introduce two additional kinds of joins that are helpful when working with certain types of complex data. Both of these allow us to join by relationships between keys that do not require values to be exactly equal. To start, let’s create two subsets of our countries data, separating out countries in Africa and in Asia, and sorting by the Gini coefficient. We remove any missing values from the output.

africa = (
    country
    .filter(c.region == "Africa")
    .select(c.full_name, c.gini)
    .sort(c.gini)
    .drop_nulls()
)
asia = (
    country
    .filter(c.region == "Asia")
    .select(c.full_name, c.gini)
    .sort(c.gini)
    .drop_nulls()
)

The function .join_asof combines two datasets by matching each row in the first (left) dataset to the nearest value in the second (right) dataset based on numeric values in a specified pair of columns. It always returns a dataset with the same number of rows as the first (left) dataset. The function is optimized to do this quickly even for large datasets; to achieve this, it requires that both datasets be sorted by the join key before being called. Below is an example where we associate each African country with the nearest Asian country in terms of their Gini coefficient. We set coalesce=False to ensure the matching country’s Gini coefficient is retained in the output.

(
    africa
    .join_asof(asia, on=c.gini, strategy="nearest", coalesce=False)
)


shape: (35, 4)



full_name
gini
full_name_right
gini_right

The original motivation for the .join_asof method was to join two time-series datasets. The idea is that we might want the current (most recent) value from one table relative to another. Hence the function’s name and the fact that it uses the backward strategy by default to find the nearest value in the second dataset that is less than (i.e., earlier than) the value in the first. You can set the strategy to forward to look in the other direction, or to nearest to find the closest value. You can also add additional keys with the by, by_left, or by_right options; these keys must match exactly before performing the nearest search. The tables must be pre-sorted by the join key(s) for the function to work.
If we need to do more exotic joins between two tables, we can use the .join_where method. It takes one or more expressions in the same format used by the .filter method to specify exactly which pairs of rows to keep in the joined dataset. To refer to columns that share the same name in both datasets, use the _right suffix to indicate the second dataset. Below is an example that finds all pairs of countries in Africa and Asia where the difference in the Gini coefficient is less than 5.

(
    africa
    .join_where(
        asia,
        (c.gini - c.gini_right).abs() < 5
    )
)

This method can be slow because it needs to manually check every combination of rows in the two datasets. However, it is very powerful and useful when combining two reasonably sized datasets in a complex way. Notice that we could obtain the same result by starting with a cross join (which returns every combination of rows) and then applying a filter. The benefit of the .join_where is that it avoids having to store in memory all combinations of non-matching rows by effectively combining the cross join and filtering into a single step.

title: 8  Combining Tables
text:
8.9 Attaching a Group Summary
In Chapter 5 we saw how .group_by() and .agg() collapse a dataset down to one row per group, and how .over() computes a group-level statistic while keeping every original row. Joins give us a second way to reach the same result as .agg() followed by a join, so it’s worth comparing them directly.
Suppose we want to attach each country’s regional average Gini coefficient to every row of country, without collapsing the table. The .over() approach does this in a single step.

(
    country
    .with_columns(
        region_avg_gini = c.gini.mean().over(c.region)
    )
)

title: 8  Combining Tables
text: References

title: 9  Restructuring Data
text:
9.1 Setup
Load the modules and datasets needed for the chapter.
import polars as pl
from plotnine import ggplot, aes
from polars import col as c

import funs

country = pl.read_csv("data/countries.csv")
cellphone = pl.read_csv("data/countries_cellphone.csv")

title: 9  Restructuring Data
text:
9.2 Introduction
The previous chapter focused on combining information stored across multiple tables. This chapter turns to a different kind of transformation: reshaping a single table into a different layout without adding or removing information. We will see that the same set of values can be arranged in several different ways, and that picking the right arrangement, rather than the right values, is often what determines whether an analysis is easy or painful.
We start by returning to the unit of observation, the idea that anchors every decision about how a table should be shaped. From there we introduce tidy data, a short set of rules that tells us when a table’s shape matches its unit of observation. The rest of the chapter puts those rules to work: pivoting and unpivoting to move between wide and long layouts, reshaping data so that it plays well with plotnine, and exploding list columns so that multiple values packed into one cell get a row each. We close by noting the one restructuring operation that does not fit this chapter’s theme, because it destroys information rather than merely rearranging it.

title: 9  Restructuring Data
text:
9.3 Units of Observation
When designing a way to store tabular data, we often must decide whether to favor many rows and few columns or many columns and few rows. A related decision, particularly when choosing a format with more columns, is what information to store in columns versus rows. In some cases the answers are clear and obvious. In others, there are trade-offs about which types of analyses will be easiest or hardest to perform with the data. Let’s work through an example.
Assume that you are a biologist tracking the heights of 100 plants over the next 14 days. There are several different ways that this data could be collected. We could have each day’s measurements in a column and each plant as a row. We could have each plant as a column and each day as a row. Or we could have a row for each combination of plant and day, with 100*14 = 1,400 rows and three columns (plant ID, day ID, and height).
A core data-science concept will help us distinguish the three cases above. A unit of observation is the entity about which data are collected and recorded in a dataset. In other words, it is the “who” or “what” that each row of the table represents. In the example with one row for each plant and one column for each day, the unit of observation is a plant. In the example with one row for each day and one column for each plant, the unit of observation is a day. In the final example, the one with 1,400 rows, we call the unit of observation plant × day (read “plant crossed with day”), which means each row corresponds to a unique combination of one plant and one day. The key takeaway is that once we have picked the unit of observation, there is only a single way to construct our data table.
Note that the concept of a unit of observation is closely linked to that of a primary key. By definition, a primary key must uniquely identify the “what” of the unit of observation. For example, a primary key for a table whose unit of observation is a plant must uniquely identify each plant. Likewise, a table with a plant × day unit of observation needs a key that uniquely identifies both the plant and the day.

title: 9  Restructuring Data
text:
9.4 Tidy Data
The plant example makes a point that is easy to miss: none of the three layouts is wrong. Each one is a complete, valid way to record the same 1,400 measurements. What differs is which unit of observation each layout is built around, and that difference has consequences for how easy the data is to work with.
Hadley Wickham’s “Tidy Data” gives a name to the layout that matches a chosen unit of observation, and a framework for recognizing it [1]. A dataset is tidy when three conditions hold:

every variable is a single column,
every observation is a single row,
every table holds a single unit of observation.

The second and third rules restate what we already know from the previous section: rows correspond to a unit of observation, and a table should stick to one such unit. The first rule is the new piece. It says that a quantity we might want to filter on, group by, or plot should live in its own column, not be smuggled into a column name, split across several columns, or bundled together with another quantity in the same cell.
The plant-by-day table with 1,400 rows is tidy for the plant × day unit of observation: plant, day, and height each get their own column, and each row is one measurement of one plant on one day. The plant-by-column table is not tidy for that same unit. Day is no longer a variable stored in a column; it has been scattered across fourteen column names, and a single observation (one plant’s full trajectory) is spread across fourteen cells instead of appearing once.
This is the detail that makes tidiness easy to misjudge: it is a property of a table relative to a unit of observation, not a property of the values themselves. The wide plant-by-day table is a perfectly tidy table for the unit of observation plant, if what you want is one row summarizing each plant’s height over the two-week window. It is only untidy once you decide the unit of observation you actually need is plant × day.
We will see this play out concretely later in the chapter with the cellphone dataset, shown below. Its unit of observation is year × country, and in this shape it is tidy: year, iso, and cell each get a column, and each row is one country’s rate in one year.

Later in this chapter we will pivot this table into a wide frame with one column per country, an entirely reasonable thing to do if the analysis calls for comparing countries directly. That wide frame will be tidy for questions about a single year, and untidy the moment we want to treat country as a variable, since at that point “country” is a set of column names rather than a column. Keep this pair of frames in mind. Understanding why the same fourteen numbers can be tidy in one shape and untidy in another is most of what you need to know to decide when a pivot is the right move.

title: 9  Restructuring Data
text:
9.6 Pivoting for plotnine
The tidy data framework was built with a particular use case in mind: it makes plotting predictable. plotnine expects long data, and once you know why, several things about earlier chapters start to make more sense.
Try mapping a plotnine aesthetic to country using cell_wide. There is no iso column to map, only a separate column for each country, so there is no sensible way to write color=c.iso. Nothing in cell_wide tells plotnine that usa and can and every other column name are all values of the same underlying variable.

(
    ggplot(cell_wide, aes(x=c.year, y=c.usa))
    .geom_line()
)

This code runs, but it only gets you a single country’s line, hardcoded by column name, and there is no way to extend it to “one line per country” without writing a separate geom_line() call for every column. Unpivoting fixes this by putting country back into the data as a variable:

cell_long = (
    cell_wide
    .unpivot(index=c.year, variable_name=c.iso, value_name=c.cell)
)

(
    cell_long
    .pipe(ggplot, aes(x=c.year, y=c.cell, color=c.iso))
    .geom_line()
)








With iso as a column, color="iso" is enough to tell plotnine to draw one line per country. This is also the answer to a question you may have had back when we first plotted cellphone by country: that plot worked on the first try, with no reshaping, because cellphone was already stored long, with one row per year × country combination. The general rule is this: before reaching for ggplot(), check whether the variable you want to map to an aesthetic (color, shape, facet, and so on) has its own column. If it does not, unpivot first.

title: 9  Restructuring Data
text:
9.7 Expand List Columns
A final type of transformation arises when multiple elements occupy a single cell in a table. This usually occurs only after a data-cleaning step we’ve performed, rather than as raw input from a CSV file. For example, the function str.split can split the text in a DataFrame column into parts. We can use this to split the multiple language codes found in the lang column of our data.

(
    country
    .select(c.full_name, c.lang)
    .with_columns(
        lang_codes = c.lang.str.split("|")
    )
)
