title: 9  Restructuring Data
text:
9.8 Round Trips Versus Aggregation
It is worth being precise about what pivoting and unpivoting do and do not do. They are round trips: pivot a long table to wide, unpivot it back, and (aside from row and column ordering) you have the original table. No values are created or destroyed, only rearranged. Aggregation is not like this. Grouping a plant × day table down to a plant table by taking the maximum height, for instance, throws away every day’s measurement except the one that set the maximum, and there is no operation that gets those measurements back. Pivoting and unpivoting change a table’s shape; aggregation changes its unit of observation, and it does so in one direction only. Keep the distinction in mind, since it is exactly what motivates the “prefer longer” advice for storing data in the first place: a long table can always be pivoted into whatever wide shape an analysis needs, but a table that has already been aggregated cannot be un-aggregated back into it.

title: 9  Restructuring Data
text:
References




[1] Hadley, W (2014 ). Tidy data. Journal of Statistical Software. 59 1–23

title: 10  Collecting Data
text:
10.1 Introduction
It’s commonly said in data science that most of our time is spent collecting and cleaning data. If we collect data in a clean format from the start, we can proceed directly to the exploration stage. Chapter 9 laid out the theory behind that claim: what makes a table tidy, and why. This chapter is about practice. Rather than the framework itself, it covers the decisions you actually face when building a dataset from scratch.
Two sources are worth reading in full. Karl Broman and Kara Woo’s “Data Organization in Spreadsheets” mixes practical advice with a discussion of the principles behind it [1]. Catherine D’Ignazio and Lauren Klein’s Data Feminism raises questions of bias, inequality, and power that come up constantly when collecting and organizing data, even in projects that look purely technical [2].
This chapter gives a concise set of recommendations for organizing and storing data, rather than an extended discussion of alternatives. For the alternatives, see the sources above. Because it doesn’t rely on advanced spreadsheet functions, any spreadsheet program will work. The screenshots come from Microsoft Excel, but the same steps apply in Google Sheets, LibreOffice, and similar programs.

title: 10  Collecting Data
text:
10.2 What Tables?
The initial step in designing storage for tabular data is deciding what different tables we will need. As we saw in Chapter 4 when discussing pivots and melts, the most important characteristic that determines the structure of a specific table is the unit of observation. Recall that the unit of observation can be a simple value (such as day or plant) or a combination of values (such as day × plant). So, we need to decide which simple and complex units of observation we need for our specific application. There are two general principles that we should keep in mind in this process:

prefer longer to wider: When we have a situation where we need to decide between a longer and wider formats, prefer the longer format. It is easier to extend, easier to check, and there is usually only one long format instead of multiple possible wide ones.
respect unit of observation: When filling in data in a table, a general rule is that we only record data that describes the entire unit of observation. This is always the case for a simple unit of observation, but can easily be broken when we have a complex key.

The second principle means that if we have a table for day × plant and need to store metadata about each plant, we should create a second table whose unit of observation is plant. This avoids copying data each time we observe a plant and prevents data inconsistencies. If we need all the data together for our analysis, it is better to merge the tables once we have collected all the data.

title: 10  Collecting Data
text:
10.3 What Variables?
The second step, after we have the units of observation associated with each table, is to decide which columns each table will contain. As discussed, selecting the columns is straightforward once we know which variables our analysis requires. It’s a good idea to include one or more columns that serve as a primary key when a natural primary key already exists in your dataset.
In addition to choosing what to put in each column, we also need to decide how the data in a column will be formatted. Again, we have a few general rules we can apply to help with this:

prefer numeric: If a variable can be stored as a number we should do so. Put other information (such as units of measurement or currency type) in another variable if needed. As much as possible, use a standard unit of measurement for a variable.
use standards: When we have character variables, the best option is to use an externally standardized format (such as USPS state codes or ISO country codes). When these do not exist, create and document your own standards as carefully as possible.
atomic cells: Only put a single thing in each variable value. Avoid, for example, putting in multiple values separated by commas. If you will ever use only part of the variable (say, the area code of a phone number) put it in a separate column.

We will return to ways to document these decisions in a data dictionary later in the chapter.

title: 10  Collecting Data
text:
10.4 What Variable Names?
The final decision we need to make regarding how to store our data is the names we’ll use for the variables. As we’ve seen, variable names in a dataset are used to describe graphics in the grammar of graphics and to manipulate data with Polars. If names are too complex or hard to remember, creating data visualizations becomes more difficult. Conversely, names that are too cryptic make it hard to recall their meaning. When variable names contain spaces or other special characters, it can be nearly impossible to work in Python without first cleaning them up after loading the data.
Here are two general rules for naming columns that we should try to follow in our work:

allowed characters: Use only lower-case letters, numbers, and underscores. The underscores can be used to represent a space between words. Also, always make sure to start the name of a variable with a lower-case letter and not a number or underscore.
simple names: Do not include extra metadata in the column names, such as units, unless they are needed to distinguish two columns. Prefer short but full names to complex abbreviations.

Note that these guidelines apply only to variable names. We can and should use spaces and capital letters in actual values where appropriate.

title: 10  Collecting Data
text:
10.5 Storing Data in Spreadsheets
Now that we have an idea of the main decisions about how to organize our data, which we will call the data schema, we will turn to the task of actually collecting our data in a spreadsheet program. As with the other sections, we can summarize the best practices with the following guidelines:

A1: Always start your data collection in the A1 cell. That is, in the upper-left corner of the spreadsheet. The first row contains the column names. The other rows contain our data.
sheets as tables: Each table should be a separate sheet. Name the sheets with the names you expect to call the data as Python objects.
just tables: Do not include any additional information in the sheet outside of the tabular format. If you need notes, include them as an extra column or extra table.
minimal formatting: Some minimal formatting that helps you enter the data can be helpful. For example, making the first row bold, sizing the column widths, using conditional formatting to highlight data errors. However, you should never use formatting to hold any actual data. Use extra columns if you need them.

Most sources on collecting data suggest saving the results from our spreadsheets in a plain-text format. This is a stripped-down representation of the data that contains no formatting information and is application-agnostic. Excel, Google Sheets, LibreOffice, and any other spreadsheet program should be able to save a dataset in a plain-text format. The most commonly used format for tabular data in data science is the comma-separated values (CSV) file. In a CSV, columns are separated by commas and each row is on its own line. The CSV format stores only a single table and therefore is not ideal for storing multiple tables at once.
Our recommendation is a bit less strict about the need to export data only as a plain-text file. Plain text is the best way to share and store a dataset once an analysis is finished, but if we will continue adding to and changing the dataset, it may be preferable to store the data as an Excel file (.xlsx) or in Google Sheets. As we have seen, the latter (Google Sheets) can be read directly into Python. This also avoids errors introduced when converting back and forth between Excel and plain-text formats. Data can be loaded directly from an Excel file as well using pl.read_excel(). Once the data collection and cleaning are finished, it’s a good idea to store the data in a plain-text format for sharing and long-term preservation.

title: 10  Collecting Data
text:
10.6 Reading Imperfect Files
Everything so far in this chapter describes how to store data well when you are the one collecting it. Most of the time, you are not. A collaborator hands you a spreadsheet with three header rows and a merged cell. A government agency publishes a CSV encoded in Windows-1252 because the export tool was built in 2003 and never updated. A survey platform codes missing responses as "-99" instead of leaving the cell blank. None of this data is unusable, but none of it will load cleanly with the default settings either. This section covers the tools for reading it anyway.
Loading a well-formed Excel file is simple. As mentioned above, pl.read_excel() reads a sheet directly into a Polars data frame:

import polars as pl

cookbooks = pl.read_excel("data/cookbooks.xlsx", sheet_name="Cookbooks")

If the file has multiple sheets and you don’t specify sheet_name, Polars returns a dictionary mapping sheet names to data frames, which is convenient when a workbook holds several related tables at once, such as the cookbooks and authors tables from the worked example below.
CSV files are just as common and, because they carry no information about types or encoding, tend to break in more varied ways. The function pl.read_csv() has a long list of arguments to handle this. In practice, a handful come up over and over:

separator: CSV technically stands for comma-separated values, but plenty of files use a semicolon, a tab, or a pipe instead, often because they were exported from a system configured for a locale where the comma is a decimal separator. Set separator=";" (or whatever character the file actually uses) to fix this.
encoding: Polars assumes UTF-8 by default. Older files, or files exported from Windows programs, are often encoded as "windows-1252" or "latin1" instead. Reading a file with the wrong encoding usually produces garbled characters rather than an outright error, so this one is easy to miss until you spot a name like “JosÃ©” where “José” should be.
skip_rows: Some export tools add a title, a generation date, or a logo caption above the actual header row. Setting skip_rows to the number of lines to discard gets you to the real start of the table.
null_values: Missing data is not always blank. Surveys and legacy databases frequently use a placeholder such as "NA", "N/A", "-99", or ".". Passing a list of these strings to null_values tells Polars to treat them as missing rather than as literal text or, worse, as numbers.
schema_overrides: Polars guesses each column’s type from its contents, and the guess is sometimes wrong in a way that loses information. A ZIP code column full of five-digit numbers gets read as an integer, which silently drops the leading zero from “02138.” Passing schema_overrides={"zip_code": pl.Utf8} forces that column to stay text.

Putting several of these together on a single file is normal, not exceptional:

survey = pl.read_csv(
    "data/survey_responses.csv",
    separator=";",
    encoding="windows-1252",
    skip_rows=2,
    null_values=["NA", "N/A", "-99"],
    schema_overrides={"zip_code": pl.Utf8, "respondent_id": pl.Utf8},
)

The messy CSV file from Chapter 2 is a good one to revisit here. It is not a contrived example; it is closer to what you should expect from real, second-hand data than the clean files we have been using for most of this book. Fixing it draws on the same skills built up across the last several chapters: recognizing the unit of observation to know which rows belong together, reshaping the result into a tidy layout once it loads, and checking the loaded data frame against the grammar-of-graphics tools from earlier chapters to confirm that a column of what looked like numbers didn’t quietly load as text. Reading an imperfect file is rarely a single function call. It’s a short investigation, and everything else in this book is what you investigate with.

title: 10  Collecting Data
text:
10.7 Data Dictionary
Finally, it is important to document exactly what information is being stored in each table and its variables. It is also important to note any relationships between primary and foreign keys, especially when they have different names. To do this, we can construct a data dictionary. Ideally, this will explain, for each variable, basic information such as the variable’s name, measurement units, and expected values or categories. Any decisions that needed to be made should also be documented. A data dictionary can be a simple text file, or it can be stored as a structured dataset. Often, when storing data in a spreadsheet program, the data dictionary is included as the first or last sheet.

title: 10  Collecting Data
text:
10.8 Special Considerations
The guidelines above summarize the primary issues to consider when designing dataset formats. Here are some additional considerations that arise from time to time:

dates: Dates and times can be particularly error-prone when building datasets. There are two general approaches that we recommend. Either record each element (year, month, day, hour, and so forth) as separate columns or use the standard formats YYYY-MM-DD and YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS, which are recommended by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO 8601).
missing values: Always use a blank entry to represent a missing value in a numeric column. This is a good approach for character variables as well, but it is also possible to record one or more special missing value codes depending on your use-case.
text and multimedia: When studying large collections of text, images, video, and other multimedia formats, often our tabular dataset contains just metadata about the records. The multimedia files (such as .mp3, .mp4 or .png) files also contain what we should consider to be data. To incorporate these, include the file name (and full path if they are not all in the same directory) as a column in one of the datasets.
synthetic keys: Sometimes we need a primary key in a table even though a good natural key does not exist. We also may want to replace a key with multiple columns with a single key. To do this, we can use an algorithm to fill in unique ids, such as a UUID.

We will see examples of all of these either in the example below or in other chapters of this text.

title: 10  Collecting Data
text:
10.9 Worked Example
We will now walk through an example of how to put these steps into action by storing information about a set of cookbooks and their authors. We’ll create two tables for this: one with the unit of analysis cookbooks and the other authors. Here is what the cookbook table might look like before any data are entered:






Figure 10.1: Example of a data table before any information other than the column names has been filled in.



Then we can fill in the values for the table:






Figure 10.2: Example of a small cookbook dataset, stored in a tidy format in Excel.



If we need to include explanatory notes for some data, we avoid abandoning the rectangular data format; instead, we add an extra notes column. For example, we note that one book is out of print in the notes column shown below. In the example table, the number of pages and the weight of that book are missing because it is out of print. To indicate this, the corresponding cells are left blank. Blank values are the only cross-software way to represent missing values consistently.






Figure 10.3: Example of a small cookbook dataset stored in a tidy format in Excel. Here, we’ve added explanatory notes in a new column.



Now let’s consider the dataset of cookbook authors. Below is an example of what the table might look like. Note that the authors table’s primary key is referenced by the books table.






Figure 10.4: A small dataset in tidy format showing properties of cookbook authors in Excel.



Finally, here is an example of a data dictionary we could use to describe the dataset:






Figure 10.5: Example of a data dictionary from the Cookbook Authors dataset.



Above, we described the data dictionary as a tabular dataset. It is also possible to present it in a less structured way, depending on how much information is required.

title: 10  Collecting Data
text:
10.10 Extensions
We have provided a consolidation of the information from the research papers mentioned in the introduction, focusing on issues of particular concern for humanities data collection. The first places to look for more information are the papers mentioned above: Karl Broman and Kara Woo’s “Data Organization in Spreadsheets” [1] and Catherine D’Ignazio and Lauren Klein’s Data Feminism [2]. Hadley Wickham’s “Tidy Data,” discussed in Chapter 9, is also worth a second look here, since its theoretical framework underlies much of the practical advice above [3]. Another source for thinking about data dictionaries, data documentation, and data publishing is the “Datasheets for Datasets” paper [4]. While the paper focuses on predictive modeling, it offers useful examples of the documentation needed when publishing large datasets and provides a critical lens on how choices made during data collection fundamentally affect subsequent data analyses.
The kinds of data that need to be collected and how to collect them ultimately depend on one’s underlying research questions. The process of translating a research question into a quantitative framework is outside the scope of this text, but it is an important consideration when working with data to address research questions in the humanities. There are many good guides to research design, though the majority focus on either scientific, hypothesis-driven quantitative designs or purely qualitative data collection. We recommend a few sources that sit at the intersection of these approaches.
Content analysis is a common social-science technique used across fields. It often mixes qualitative questions with quantitative data, making it a good source of research-design advice for humanities data analysis [5] [6] [7]. Similarly, corpus linguistics sits at the boundary of the humanities and the social sciences and offers many resources on best practices [8]. Corpus linguistics often works with both textual and audio data and has developed specific techniques for working with the rich, multimodal datasets considered in the following chapters. Finally, sociology is another field that mixes humanistic questions with quantitative data, providing additional references on research design [9]. Although these references include domain-specific elements, many general principles can be extended to other domains.
Finally, notice that we did not start the book with data collection, even though collecting data is most often the first step in data analysis. We saved this until the last core chapter because understanding how to collect and store data often goes hand in hand with understanding how to visualize, organize, and restructure it. A deeper understanding of how to collect data—particularly data with complex components such as text, networks, and images—will develop as we work through the remaining chapters.

title: 10  Collecting Data
text:
References




[1] Broman, K W and Woo, K H (2018 ). Data organization in spreadsheets. The American Statistician. Taylor & Francis. 72 2–10


[2] D’ignazio, C and Klein, L F (2020 ). Data Feminism. MIT press


[3] Hadley, W (2014 ). Tidy data. Journal of Statistical Software. 59 1–23


[4] Gebru, T, Morgenstern, J, Vecchione, B, Vaughan, J W, Wallach, H, Iii, H D and Crawford, K (2021 ). Datasheets for datasets. Communications of the ACM. ACM New York, NY, USA. 64 86–92


[5] Schreier, M (2012 ). Qualitative content analysis in practice. Qualitative content analysis in practice. Sage. 1–280


[6] Neuendorf, K A (2017 ). The Content Analysis Guidebook. Sage publications


[7] Krippendorff, K (2018 ). Content Analysis: An Introduction to Its Methodology. Sage publications


[8] Paquot, M and Gries, S T (2021 ). A Practical Handbook of Corpus Linguistics. Springer Nature


[9] Bernard, H R (2017 ). Research Methods in Anthropology: Qualitative and Quantitative Approaches. Rowman & Littlefield

title: 11  Spatial Data
text:
11.1 Setup
Load the modules and datasets needed for the chapter. Importing funs registers a namespace called geo on every Polars DataFrame, which is how we will do all of the spatial work below.
import polars as pl
from plotnine import ggplot, aes
from polars import col as c

import funs

metro = pl.read_csv("data/acs_cbsa.csv")

title: 11  Spatial Data
text:
11.2 Introduction
Every dataset we have worked with so far in this book has been detached from physical space. GDP figures and blood pressure readings are just numbers; it makes no difference to the analysis where they were recorded. A lot of data is not like that. Crime reports happen at street addresses and asthma rates vary by neighborhood. A hospital sits at a fixed point that is some measurable distance from every school in a city. Answering questions about this kind of data requires treating location as a geometric object with shape, area, and spatial relationships to other objects, not as two more numeric columns.
Spatial analysis attaches a geometry to each observation: a mathematical description of a location or region on the Earth’s surface. A geometry can be a single point (the location of a coffee shop) or a polygon with thousands of vertices (the boundary of a congressional district). Once each row has a geometry, we can ask questions that have no equivalent for ordinary tables. Which points fall inside which polygons? Which polygons share a border? Which observation is nearest to each other observation?
Polars has no built-in support for geometry. The standard Python library for spatial work is GeoPandas, which is built on pandas rather than Polars. Instead of asking you to convert between the two libraries by hand, the funs module registers a namespace called geo on every Polars DataFrame. Any GeoPandas method or property can be reached through it: metro.geo.to_crs(5069) reprojects the data, state.geo.area computes areas, and metro.geo.sjoin(state) performs a spatial join. Each call converts the table to GeoPandas, runs the operation, and converts the result back to Polars. Two conventions make the round trip work. Geometries are stored as plain text in a column named geometry, using a standard called Well-Known Text (WKT), and the coordinate system is recorded in a helper column named _crs. Both columns will show up in the printed output throughout the chapter.

title: 11  Spatial Data
text:
11.3 Spatial Points
The simplest type of spatial data consists of points: individual locations specified by their coordinates. Points can represent cities, sensor stations, retail locations, or anything else that can reasonably be reduced to a single spot on the map. Each point is defined by two numbers, a longitude (the east-west position) and a latitude (the north-south position).
We begin with a dataset describing metropolitan statistical areas (MSAs) in the United States. Each row is a single metro region, with columns for population, economic indicators, and geographic coordinates.

Because longitude and latitude are just numbers, we can make a basic plot without any spatial tools at all. The longitude values go on the x-axis and the latitude values on the y-axis.

(
    ggplot(metro, aes(c.lon, c.lat))
    .geom_point()
)








This scatter plot works, but it has problems. It treats longitude and latitude as if they were ordinary Cartesian coordinates, when they actually describe positions on a curved surface. The distortion gets worse at higher latitudes, where equal increments in longitude correspond to shorter physical distances. The plot also gives the viewer nothing to orient by: no coastlines and no state boundaries.
To go further, we need to turn the latitude and longitude columns into proper geometries. In the WKT standard, a point is written as text in the form POINT (lon lat), so nothing stops us from building the geometry column ourselves with pl.format.

metro.with_columns(
    geometry = pl.format("POINT ({} {})", c.lon, c.lat)
)

title: 11  Spatial Data
text:
11.4 Coordinate Systems (CRS)
One of the most important concepts in spatial analysis is the coordinate reference system (CRS), sometimes called a spatial reference system. A CRS defines how coordinates map to actual locations on Earth. This might seem straightforward, but Earth’s surface is curved while maps are flat. Any attempt to represent the globe on a two-dimensional surface involves trade-offs: you can preserve shapes or areas or distances, but never all three at once.
The most common CRS for storing geographic data is WGS 84 (World Geodetic System 1984), which uses latitude and longitude measured in degrees. It is the system used by GPS devices and most web mapping services, and it is what points_from_xy recorded for us above; crs=4326 is its default. For visualization and for many calculations, however, we usually want to project the data into a different CRS that is optimized for a particular region or purpose.
Each CRS is identified by a numeric code in the EPSG (European Petroleum Survey Group) registry. For example:

EPSG:4326 is WGS 84, the standard latitude/longitude system
EPSG:5069 is NAD83 Conus Albers, an equal-area projection suitable for the contiguous United States
EPSG:3857 is Web Mercator, used by Google Maps and other web mapping services
EPSG:32618 is UTM Zone 18N, appropriate for the US East Coast
EPSG:2154 is the official projection for Metropolitan France (including Corsica), mandated by French law
EPSG:7791 is Italy’s modern national reference system and the most accurate for Italian data

To find the appropriate EPSG code for a specific region or purpose, you can search the EPSG.io website or consult the Spatial Reference database. Both let you look up projections by name, region, or properties (such as whether they preserve area or shape).
The to_crs method transforms the coordinates from the current CRS into a new one. Because the geometry column is understood by plotnine as well, we can pass the reprojected table straight into ggplot and draw it with geom_map. The coord_fixed call keeps one map unit on the x-axis the same length as one on the y-axis, which matters now that the coordinates are real distances.

(
    ggplot(metro.geo.to_crs(5069))
    .geom_map()
    .coord_fixed()
)








The shape of the point cloud now looks much more like the familiar outline of the continental United States. Alaska and Hawaii, if present in the data, would appear distorted under this projection because it is optimized for the lower 48 states.






NoteWhy projections matter





The choice of projection affects calculations, not just appearances. Consider calculating the distance between two cities. In WGS 84 coordinates, the distance between two points depends on their latitude: one degree of longitude represents about 111 kilometers at the equator but only about 85 kilometers at 40° latitude (roughly the latitude of New York). If you compute distances using raw latitude/longitude values as if they were Cartesian coordinates, your results will be systematically wrong.
Similarly, if you want to calculate the area of a state or country, you need an equal-area projection. Using a projection that distorts areas (like Web Mercator) will give incorrect results. This is why Greenland appears larger than Africa on many web maps, even though Africa is actually 14 times larger.

title: 11  Spatial Data
text:
11.5 Interactive Visualization
Static maps work well in publications and reports, but interactive maps allow for exploration. GeoPandas provides an explore method that builds an interactive map from the Folium library, which in turn uses Leaflet.js. Because explore is an ordinary GeoPandas method, the geo namespace picks it up like any other.

metro.geo.explore(tooltip=["name"])

Make this Notebook Trusted to load map: File -> Trust Notebook


The tooltip parameter lists the columns to display when the user hovers over a point. Note that it takes plain column names as strings; the c. shorthand works inside Polars expressions and aes(), but here the names are handed directly to GeoPandas. Try zooming in on different regions and clicking on individual metro areas to see their names. The base map provides the geographic context that was missing from our static scatter plot.

title: 11  Spatial Data
text:
11.6 Spatial Polygons
While points are useful for representing discrete locations, many spatial phenomena are better described by polygons: closed shapes that define the boundaries of regions. States, countries, zip codes, census tracts, and watersheds are all naturally represented as polygons. Each polygon is a series of coordinate pairs that trace its boundary.
Spatial data is commonly stored in specialized file formats that can encode complex geometries along with their attributes. The GeoJSON format is particularly popular for web applications because it is based on JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) and can be read by virtually any programming language. GeoJSON files have a standardized structure in which each row of the equivalent table is called a feature, and all features are collected into a FeatureCollection. The official specification is maintained by the Internet Engineering Task Force as RFC 7946.
Other common formats include Shapefiles (a legacy format still widely used), GeoPackage (a modern SQLite-based format), and database formats like PostGIS. The funs.read_file function can read most of them, returning a Polars DataFrame with the geometries already converted to WKT.

state = funs.read_file("data/acs_state.geojson")
state

The geometry column now contains polygons rather than points. Each polygon is defined by many coordinate pairs, sometimes thousands for states with complex coastlines, all written out in the WKT strings.
Unlike point data, polygon data cannot be meaningfully drawn with a scatter plot; we need geom_map, which knows how to trace shapes from their vertex coordinates. The effect of projection is also much more noticeable with polygons than with points.

(
    ggplot(state.geo.to_crs(5069))
    .geom_map(fill="white", color="black")
    .coord_fixed()
)








Compare this to what the same data would look like in unprojected coordinates. The familiar shapes of states would be stretched, particularly in the northern regions where lines of longitude converge. The Albers projection compresses and expands the coordinates to give a more faithful picture of the true shapes and relative sizes of states.
Interactive maps work with polygons just as they do with points. Users can hover over states to see their names and other attributes.

state.geo.explore(tooltip=["name"])

Make this Notebook Trusted to load map: File -> Trust Notebook








NoteOther geometry types





Points and polygons are the most common geometry types, but GeoJSON and other spatial formats support several others:

LineString: A sequence of connected points forming a path, useful for roads, rivers, or flight routes
MultiPoint: A collection of points treated as a single feature
MultiLineString: Multiple disconnected line segments treated as a single feature
MultiPolygon: Multiple disconnected polygons treated as a single feature (useful for states with islands, like Hawaii)
GeometryCollection: A heterogeneous collection containing any mix of the above types

Most of the techniques in this chapter apply to all geometry types, though some operations (like calculating area) only make sense for polygons.

title: 11  Spatial Data
text:
11.7 Polygon Metrics
Once we have polygons, we can compute geometric properties from them. Two useful ones are the centroid (the geometric center of a polygon) and the area (the amount of surface the boundary encloses). Both are only meaningful in a projected CRS; computed on raw latitude and longitude they come out as nonsense numbers in squared degrees. So we first project the states and keep the projected version from here on.

state = state.geo.to_crs(5069)

In GeoPandas, centroid is a property rather than a method, so it is accessed without parentheses. Because the result is a new set of geometries, the geo namespace writes them into the geometry column of the returned table.

state.geo.centroid

Each state’s polygon has been replaced by a single point at its center. Since we did not assign the result back, state itself still holds the original polygons. For roughly rectangular states like Colorado, the centroid falls near the geographic center. For states with irregular shapes or several disconnected parts, the centroid can land outside the state’s actual boundary.
The area property returns ordinary numbers rather than geometries, so the namespace appends them as a new column named after the property.

state = state.geo.area
state.sort(c.area).select(c.name, c.area)

title: 11  Spatial Data
text:
11.8 Choropleth Maps
A choropleth map colors geographic regions according to the value of a variable. This kind of plot appears everywhere quantities vary across space: election results by county, income levels by census tract, or disease rates by state. With the area column now part of the table, building one takes a single aesthetic mapping.

(
    ggplot(state, aes(fill=c.area))
    .geom_map(color="white")
    .coord_fixed()
)








The same idea works interactively. The column parameter tells explore which variable to use for coloring, and a legend shows how colors correspond to values.

state.geo.explore(column="area", tooltip=["name"])

Make this Notebook Trusted to load map: File -> Trust Notebook


Choropleth maps are useful but easy to misread. Larger regions occupy more visual space, so they dominate the viewer’s attention even when they contain fewer people or matter less for the question at hand. For this reason, choropleths work best for variables that are naturally area-based (like population density) rather than absolute counts (like total population).

title: 11  Spatial Data
text:
11.9 Spatial Joins
In Chapter 9, we learned how to join two tables based on a shared key column. Spatial joins extend this idea by joining tables based on geometric relationships. Instead of matching rows where a key value is equal, we match rows where geometries intersect, touch, contain, or sit within some distance of each other.
The most common spatial join matches points to the polygons that contain them. Given our metro areas (points) and states (polygons), we can find which state contains each metro area. Spatial operations require both tables to be in the same CRS, and state is now in EPSG:5069, so we first project the metro points to match.

metro = metro.geo.to_crs(5069)
metro.geo.sjoin(state)

The result contains the columns from both tables, with each metro area matched to the state it falls inside. Column names that appear in both tables get _left and _right suffixes, and GeoPandas adds an index_right column recording which row of the right table matched. Metro areas that straddle a state boundary are assigned to whichever state contains their center point, since that is the coordinate we used to build the geometry. Notice that the geometry in the output is the point geometry from the first (left) table.
We can also reverse the order of the join to keep the state geometry in the result. With the states first, the output contains polygons, and each state is matched to the metro areas it contains.

state.geo.sjoin(metro)

This produces more rows than we have states because a state can contain many metro areas. The logic is analogous to a one-to-many relational join: the state geometry is duplicated once for each metro area inside it.
Spatial joins can test various predicates (spatial relationships) beyond simple containment. The predicate parameter chooses which relationship to use. For example, we can find which states share a border by joining the state table to itself with the touches predicate.

state.geo.sjoin(state, predicate="touches")

This self-join matches each state to every other state that shares at least one point on its boundary, giving all pairs of neighboring states. Each border appears twice: California touches Oregon, and Oregon touches California. If you only want each pair once, you need to filter or deduplicate the results.
Other useful predicates include:

intersects: Geometries share any space (including boundaries)
contains: The first geometry completely encloses the second
within: The first geometry is completely enclosed by the second
crosses: Geometries share some but not all interior points
overlaps: Geometries share some space but neither contains the other

title: 11  Spatial Data
text:
11.10 Nearest Neighbor Joins
Sometimes we want to match features not by overlap or containment but by proximity. The sjoin_nearest method finds, for each feature in the first table, the closest feature in the second. It answers questions like “what is the nearest hospital to each school?” or “which weather station is closest to each measurement site?”

metro.geo.sjoin_nearest(metro, exclusive=True, distance_col="distance")

Here we join the metro table to itself to find each metro area’s nearest neighbor. The exclusive=True parameter prevents each metro area from matching itself, which would always be a distance of zero, and distance_col adds a column with the distance to the match. Because the data is in the Albers projection, those distances are in meters. This is another place where projecting first pays off: in raw latitude and longitude the distances would come out in degrees.
Nearest neighbor joins are computationally expensive because they potentially compare every feature in the first table to every feature in the second. GeoPandas uses spatial index structures (R-trees) to prune most of these comparisons, but the operation still scales worse than a simple predicate join, so expect it to be slow on large datasets.

title: 11  Spatial Data
text:
11.11 Buffers
A buffer expands a geometry outward by a fixed distance, turning each point into a disc and each polygon into a slightly larger polygon. Buffers are how we answer “within X of” questions: the region within 100 kilometers of a metro center is exactly the 100 kilometer buffer around its point. Because buffer returns new geometries, the namespace writes them into the geometry column, just as centroid did. The distance is measured in the units of the current CRS, so with our data in the Albers projection the argument below means 100,000 meters. This is one more reason the earlier projection step matters: buffering unprojected data by 100,000 would ask for discs 100,000 degrees across.

(
    ggplot(metro.geo.buffer(100_000))
    .geom_map(alpha=0.3)
    .coord_fixed()
)








The translucent fill makes the overlaps visible. The discs pile up into a nearly solid band along the eastern seaboard and thin out to isolated circles across the Mountain West, a picture of urban density that the plain point map only hints at.
Buffers become more interesting in combination with spatial joins. Each buffered metro is now a polygon, so joining it against the states finds every state within 100 kilometers of a metro center rather than only the state that contains it.

metro.geo.buffer(100_000).geo.sjoin(state)

Metro areas near a state line now match two or more states, which is often exactly what an analysis needs. A hospital planning study, for example, cares about everyone within driving distance, not just residents on one side of a border.

title: 11  Spatial Data
text:
11.12 Dissolving Boundaries
The dissolve method is the spatial counterpart of the group_by and agg pattern we have used throughout the book. It collects rows into groups, merges each group’s geometries into one, and erases the internal boundaries between them. Merging states into larger regions is the classic example.
Our state table has no region column, so we will build one, which gives us an excuse to compose several namespace operations. The Albers projection we have been using is centered on the 96th meridian, meaning a centroid with a negative x coordinate sits west of 96°W and a positive one sits east of it. The x property of a point geometry is available through the namespace like everything else: state.geo.centroid swaps the polygons for their center points, .geo.x appends the x coordinate as a column, and get_column pulls out just that column so we can attach it to the original polygon table.

state = state.with_columns(
    state.geo.centroid.geo.x.get_column("x")
).with_columns(
    side = pl.when(c.x < 0).then(pl.lit("West")).otherwise(pl.lit("East"))
)
state.select(c.name, c.x, c.side)

With the grouping column in place, dissolve works like a spatial group_by. The by parameter names the group key and aggfunc says how to combine the ordinary columns; here we sum the state areas within each group. The group key comes back as a regular column because the namespace moves the pandas index into the table during the return trip.

region = state.geo.dissolve(by="side", aggfunc={"area": "sum"})

title: 11  Spatial Data
text:
11.13 Exploding Geometries
The explode method goes in the opposite direction from dissolve: it splits every multi-part geometry into one row per part. States made of disconnected pieces, like Hawaii, become several rows, while single-polygon states pass through unchanged. The ordinary columns, including the state-level area we computed earlier, are simply copied onto every part.

state.geo.explode().filter(c.name == "Hawaii")

title: 11  Spatial Data
text:
11.14 Simplifying Boundaries
Coastlines and rivers make state boundaries expensive to store and draw. The count_coordinates method reports how many vertices each geometry uses, and the totals are surprisingly large.

(
    state
    .geo.count_coordinates()
    .select(c.name, c.count_coordinates)
    .sort(c.count_coordinates, descending=True)
)

Alaska, with its fjords and island chains, sits far ahead of everything else. Most of that detail is invisible in a plot of the whole country, so we can trade it away for speed with simplify. The method removes vertices while keeping the boundary within a tolerance of its original position, measured in CRS units. The 20 kilometer tolerance below is deliberately coarse so that the effect shows up on the page; the states turn angular but stay recognizable.

(
    ggplot(state.geo.simplify(20_000))
    .geom_map(fill="white", color="black")
    .coord_fixed()
)








For real work a much smaller tolerance, on the order of the width of a plotted line, cuts vertex counts dramatically without any visible change. One caveat: each geometry is simplified independently, so neighboring states can develop small gaps or overlaps along their shared borders. That rarely matters for plotting, but it can affect spatial joins that use predicates like touches, so simplify for display rather than for analysis.

title: 11  Spatial Data
text:
11.15 Best Practices
Working with spatial data introduces several sources of error that do not arise with ordinary tables.
CRS mismatches: If two datasets use different coordinate reference systems, spatial operations will give incorrect results. Points can appear in the wrong location, or spatial joins can fail to find matches that are really there. Verify that your datasets are in compatible CRSs before performing spatial operations, and transform them to a common CRS with to_crs if they are not.
Unprojected coordinates for calculations: Never calculate distances or areas using raw latitude/longitude coordinates as if they were Cartesian. The errors can be substantial, especially for features that span large areas or sit far from the equator. Project the data to an appropriate CRS first.
Polygon validity: Complex polygons can become geometrically invalid through self-intersections or other topological errors. Many spatial functions fail or give wrong answers on invalid geometries. If you hit strange errors, check whether your geometries are valid and repair them if necessary.
Boundary effects: Features that span the boundary between regions can cause problems for spatial joins. A metro area straddling a state line gets assigned to one state or the other depending on where its center point falls. Be aware of these edge cases and handle them deliberately in your analysis.

title: 11  Spatial Data
text: References

title: 12  Data APIs and Corpora
text:
12.1 Setup
Load the modules needed for the chapter. We will use the requests and requests-cache modules to make API calls over the network.
import time
from pathlib import Path
from urllib.parse import quote

import polars as pl
from plotnine import ggplot, aes
from polars import col as c

import funs

import requests
import requests_cache

title: 12  Data APIs and Corpora
text:
12.2 Introduction
Every dataset in the book so far arrived as a file: something we could read with pl.read_csv and inspect in full before writing a line of analysis code. A lot of interesting data does not come packaged that way. It sits on remote servers and is only available through a programmatic interface, either because it changes too often to distribute as a static file or because the provider wants to control how it is accessed. Weather forecasts, library catalogs, legislative records, and the entire edit history of Wikipedia all fall in this category.
This chapter has two goals. The first is to teach the mechanics of application programming interfaces (APIs): how to send an HTTP request, read the response codes, page through large result sets, and turn nested JSON into the flat tables we know how to work with. The second goal is to actually build something with those mechanics. Over the course of the chapter we will assemble a corpus of Wikipedia articles about nineteenth-century British and Irish novelists, collecting each article’s metadata, full text, internal links, revision history, daily page views, and links to versions in other languages. The chapters that follow on temporal, textual, and network data all analyze this corpus, so the output here is not a throwaway example. It is a small, documented data pipeline, and the decisions we make while building it (how the sample is defined, how redirects are handled, which identifiers we key on, when the data was retrieved) will matter every time the data gets used later.
We work with Wikipedia for a practical reason: its APIs are free, require no account or key, and expose an unusually wide range of data types from a single source. The same request patterns apply to almost any HTTP-based API you will meet in practice.

title: 12  Data APIs and Corpora
text:
12.3 APIs and HTTP Requests
An API is a generic term for an interface that two computer programs can communicate across. The most common type for data access communicates over the Hypertext Transfer Protocol (HTTP), the same protocol your browser uses to fetch web pages. When you visit a website, your browser sends an HTTP request and receives a response containing the page content. Data APIs work the same way, except that instead of returning a human-readable page, the server returns structured data, usually in JSON format, designed for programs to consume.
HTTP defines several methods that describe what kind of action a request performs. The most common are GET (retrieve data), POST (send data to create something new), PUT (update existing data), and DELETE (remove data). For data collection we almost always use GET. Each request goes to a specific URL called an endpoint, usually with a set of parameters attached that specify exactly what we want. The server replies with a numeric status code indicating what happened: 200 means success, 404 means the requested resource was not found, 429 means we are sending requests too quickly, and codes in the 500s mean the server itself hit an error. Checking status codes is the first step in debugging any failed request.
Many APIs require an account and an API key, a unique string that authenticates your requests, and some charge per request. The APIs we use in this chapter are run by the Wikimedia Foundation and require neither. We will work with two of them. The MediaWiki Action API at https://en.wikipedia.org/w/api.php is the workhorse: it can return page metadata, article text, links, revision histories, and much more, all controlled through URL parameters. Its documentation is at https://www.mediawiki.org/wiki/API:Main_page. The Wikimedia REST API at https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/ serves analytics data such as page view counts through a different URL structure that we will look at near the end of the chapter. Python’s requests library handles both; its documentation is at https://requests.readthedocs.io/.

title: 12  Data APIs and Corpora
text:
12.4 Caching Responses
Before making any requests, we set up a local cache that stores every response we receive. The requests_cache library provides a drop-in replacement for a standard requests session that saves responses in a SQLite database file and returns the saved copy whenever the same request is repeated.

session = requests_cache.CachedSession(
    cache_name="data/wiki/requests_cache",
    backend="sqlite",
    allowable_methods=("GET", "HEAD", "POST"),
    expire_after=None
)

The cache matters for three reasons. It avoids hammering the server with repeated requests for the same data, which the Wikimedia Foundation explicitly asks clients to avoid. It makes development fast, since re-running a notebook pulls everything from disk instead of the network. And, most useful for our purposes, the cache file is a permanent record of the exact bytes the server sent us. Setting expire_after=None means entries never expire, so the SQLite file becomes the raw layer of our pipeline: every table we build later in the chapter can be reconstructed from it without touching the network at all. We will return to this idea when we discuss reproducibility at the end of the chapter.

title: 12  Data APIs and Corpora
text:
12.5 A First Request
An API call needs three things: the endpoint URL, a dictionary of parameters, and a set of headers identifying who we are. The parameters are specific to each API, so consulting the documentation is unavoidable. The User-Agent header identifies your application and gives server administrators a way to contact you if your requests cause problems. Wikimedia requires a descriptive one; requests with generic or missing user agents may be blocked.

base_url = "https://en.wikipedia.org/w/api.php"
headers = {
    "User-Agent": "DataScienceBook/1.0 (tarnold2@richmond.edu)"
}

params = {
    "action": "query",
    "format": "json",
    "prop": "info",
    "titles": "Emily Brontë"
}

response = session.get(base_url, params=params, headers=headers)
response.status_code

200


The action parameter tells the Action API what kind of operation we want (query retrieves data), format asks for JSON, prop names the property to fetch (info returns basic page metadata), and titles names the article. The status code of 200 confirms the request succeeded. The response body is JSON, which the response object can parse directly into Python dictionaries and lists.

data = response.json()
data["query"]["pages"]

{'9810': {'pageid': 9810,
  'ns': 0,
  'title': 'Emily Brontë',
  'contentmodel': 'wikitext',
  'pagelanguage': 'en',
  'pagelanguagehtmlcode': 'en',
  'pagelanguagedir': 'ltr',
  'touched': '2026-07-12T06:01:37Z',
  'lastrevid': 1363711038,
  'length': 72283}}


Here we see a pattern that recurs constantly with APIs: the useful data is nested several levels deep, inside data["query"]["pages"], and is keyed by the page’s numeric ID rather than arranged as rows. We navigate to the nested records, pull out the fields we want as a list of dictionaries, and hand that list to Polars.

rows = []
for page in data["query"]["pages"].values():
    rows.append({
        "page_id": page["pageid"],
        "title": page["title"],
        "length": page["length"],
        "touched": page["touched"]
    })

pl.DataFrame(rows)


shape: (1, 4)



page_id
title
length
touched


i64
str
i64
str




9810
"Emily Brontë"
72283
"2026-07-12T06:01:37Z"






The length field is the size of the article source in bytes and touched records when the page last changed. Every API nests its responses differently, so there is no single recipe. The skill to practice is exploring the structure (print intermediate pieces, read the documentation) and then writing a short loop that flattens exactly the fields you need.

title: 12  Data APIs and Corpora
text:
12.6 Being a Polite Client
Wikimedia’s APIs are free, and the etiquette they ask for in return is modest: send a real User-Agent, make requests serially rather than in parallel, and cache what you receive. There is also a formal rate limit, a cap on how many requests a client may send per unit of time. Exceed it and the server starts answering with status code 429 (“too many requests”) instead of data, at which point the correct behavior is to wait and retry rather than to push harder. Commercial APIs tend to have much stricter limits than Wikimedia’s, and hitting them repeatedly can get a key revoked, so pausing between requests is a habit worth building early.
Since every request in the rest of the chapter uses the same session and headers, we wrap the pattern in a helper function. It raises an error on any failing status code and sleeps briefly after each request that actually went over the network. Cached responses skip the pause, which is what makes re-running the chapter fast.
One detail matters more than it looks: clearing the session’s cookies before every request. Wikimedia’s responses carry Vary: Cookie, and the rotating session cookies it sets (WMF-Uniq, NetworkProbeLimit, and similar) would otherwise accumulate in session.cookies and get resent on later requests. Because requests_cache honors Vary headers by comparing the cookies attached to the current request against the cookies attached when a response was first cached, and those values differ on every run, a stale cookie jar silently defeats the entire cache: expire_after=None looks like it should make every rerun instant, but each request still fails its Vary: Cookie check and goes back out over the network. Since this API needs no cookies to work, clearing them keeps every request’s cookie state identical (empty) across runs, so cached entries actually get reused.

def polite_get(url, params=None):
    session.cookies.clear()
    response = session.get(url, params=params, headers=headers)
    response.raise_for_status()
    if not getattr(response, "from_cache", False):
        time.sleep(0.2)
    return response

title: 12  Data APIs and Corpora
text:
12.7 Defining the Population
Now we turn to the corpus itself, and the first decision is the one that shapes everything downstream: which pages are we collecting? This is a sampling question, and APIs make it easy to get wrong because they make it easy to grab whatever is convenient. A defensible corpus starts from an explicit definition of the population, written down where future users of the data can find it.
There are several reasonable ways to define a set of Wikipedia pages. You can curate a list by hand, which gives full control but does not scale and encodes your own blind spots. You can query the Wikidata knowledge graph for entities matching structured criteria such as occupation and birth country. Or you can use Wikipedia’s own category system, in which editors tag each article with subject labels. We take the category route here because it stays within the API we have already met, and because its imperfections are instructive.
Our population is defined as the article pages belonging to two categories: Category:Victorian novelists and Category:19th-century Irish novelists. Together these give a corpus of nineteenth-century British and Irish novelists that is large enough to be interesting and small enough to collect in one sitting. Two caveats belong in the record. First, categories are maintained by volunteer editors, so membership reflects tagging practice as much as historical fact; an author missing the tag is invisible to us. Second, we collect only direct members and do not descend into subcategories, a deliberate choice that keeps the definition simple at the cost of some coverage.
The Action API returns category members through list=categorymembers. A single response holds at most 500 results, so retrieving a full category requires pagination: when more results remain, the response includes a continue block whose values we feed back into the parameters of the next request, repeating until the block disappears.

def category_members(category):
    members = []
    params = {
        "action": "query",
        "format": "json",
        "list": "categorymembers",
        "cmtitle": category,
        "cmtype": "page",
        "cmlimit": "max"
    }
    while True:
        data = polite_get(base_url, params).json()
        members.extend(data["query"]["categorymembers"])
        if "continue" not in data:
            break
        params.update(data["continue"])
    return members

Setting cmtype to page filters out subcategories and files, and cmlimit set to "max" asks for the largest batch the server allows. We run the function over both categories and stack the results into a table. A handful of authors appear in both categories, so we deduplicate on the page ID, keeping the first category we saw for each.

categories = [
    "Category:Victorian novelists",
    "Category:19th-century Irish novelists"
]

rows = []
for category in categories:
    for member in category_members(category):
        rows.append({
            "page_id": member["pageid"],
            "title": member["title"],
            "category": category
        })

authors = (
    pl.DataFrame(rows)
    .unique(subset="page_id", keep="first")
    .sort(c.title)
)
authors

title: 12  Data APIs and Corpora
text:
12.8 Redirects and Missing Pages
Our population came from category listings, which hand us canonical titles and page IDs directly. Corpora built from a hand-curated list start from titles typed by a person, and those are far less reliable. Wikipedia handles the mess through redirects: the page “Charlotte Bronte”, without the diaeresis, is not an article but a pointer that forwards readers to “Charlotte Brontë”. Dozens of alternate spellings, pen names, and former titles resolve this way. A title with no article and no redirect is simply missing.
The Action API can resolve redirects for us if we pass redirects=1, and it batches nicely: up to 50 titles can go in a single request, separated by pipe characters. Here is a small demonstration with one redirect, one canonical title, and one title that does not exist.

params = {
    "action": "query",
    "format": "json",
    "prop": "info",
    "redirects": 1,
    "titles": "Charlotte Bronte|Oscar Wilde|Not A Real Author Page"
}
data = polite_get(base_url, params).json()
data["query"].get("redirects")

[{'from': 'Charlotte Bronte', 'to': 'Charlotte Brontë'}]


The redirects block records each substitution the server made, mapping the title we asked for to the title we got. Keeping this mapping is worth the trouble: if your source list says “Charlotte Bronte” and your corpus says “Charlotte Brontë”, the mapping is the only thing that connects them. Missing pages show up in the pages block flagged with a missing key and a negative placeholder ID.

{k: v for k, v in data["query"]["pages"].items() if "missing" in v}

{'-1': {'ns': 0,
  'title': 'Not A Real Author Page',
  'missing': '',
  'contentmodel': 'wikitext',
  'pagelanguage': 'en',
  'pagelanguagehtmlcode': 'en',
  'pagelanguagedir': 'ltr'}}


A missing title should never be silently dropped. It might be a typo worth fixing, a person who has no article (itself informative about coverage), or a sign that your source list and Wikipedia disagree about a name. Log them, look at them, and record the decision.
This example also raises the question of which identifier to key the corpus on, because the options differ in stability. Titles are the least stable: pages get renamed, and yesterday’s title becomes today’s redirect. The numeric page_id survives renames and is stable within a single wiki, which is why our authors table uses it as the primary key. The most stable identifier of all is the revision ID, which names one immutable version of one page; we will meet it in the revision history section and again when we discuss snapshots.

title: 12  Data APIs and Corpora
text:
12.9 Article Text
With the population fixed, we start attaching data to it, beginning with the article text that the later chapter on textual data will analyze. The TextExtracts property of the Action API returns article text with the wiki markup stripped away. Setting explaintext gives plain text rather than HTML. The one quirk is that full-length extracts are limited to a single page per request, so this step is a loop over the corpus rather than a batched call. On the first run it is the slow part of the chapter; on every later run the cache answers instantly.

rows = []
for page_id in authors["page_id"]:
    params = {
        "action": "query",
        "format": "json",
        "prop": "extracts",
        "explaintext": 1,
        "pageids": page_id
    }
    data = polite_get(base_url, params).json()
    page = data["query"]["pages"][str(page_id)]
    rows.append({"page_id": page_id, "text": page.get("extract", "")})

texts = pl.DataFrame(rows)
texts

A quick look at the opening of one article confirms the format: readable prose with section headings marked by equals signs, and no citations, tables, or infoboxes.

print(texts.row(0, named=True)["text"][:400])

Alfred Edward Woodley Mason (7 May 1865 – 22 November 1948) was an English author and Liberal Party Member of Parliament. He is best remembered for his 1902 novel of courage and cowardice in wartime, The Four Feathers, and is also known as the creator of Inspector Hanaud, a French detective who was an early template for Agatha Christie's famous Hercule Poirot.
His prolific output in short stories 


Extracts are a processed view of the article, which is exactly what we want for text analysis. If a project instead needs the raw wiki markup or the rendered HTML, the API provides both (through prop=revisions with rvprop=content, or the page HTML endpoints of the REST API), and the loop above transfers with minor changes.

title: 12  Data APIs and Corpora
text:
12.10 Links Between Pages
Wikipedia articles link to each other constantly, and those links form a network: Charlotte Brontë’s article links to Elizabeth Gaskell’s, who wrote her biography. The chapter on network data will study this structure, so here we collect each article’s outgoing internal links. The links property returns them, restricted by plnamespace=0 to links pointing at ordinary articles rather than talk pages or categories. Link lists can be long, so the same continue pagination we used for category members shows up again.

rows = []
for page_id in authors["page_id"]:
    params = {
        "action": "query",
        "format": "json",
        "prop": "links",
        "plnamespace": 0,
        "pllimit": "max",
        "pageids": page_id
    }
    while True:
        data = polite_get(base_url, params).json()
        page = data["query"]["pages"][str(page_id)]
        for link in page.get("links", []):
            rows.append({"page_id": page_id, "link_title": link["title"]})
        if "continue" not in data:
            break
        params.update(data["continue"])

links = pl.DataFrame(rows)
links

Most targets are outside the corpus (places, novels, publishers), which is fine; the table records everything, and narrowing it is a join. Matching the target titles against the authors table keeps only the author-to-author edges and attaches the target’s page ID.

links_internal = (

title: 12  Data APIs and Corpora
text:
12.11 Revision Histories
Every Wikipedia article carries its complete edit history, and that history is a time series: when was the page created, when did editing surge, how did the article grow? The later chapter on temporal data starts from exactly this table. The revisions property returns the history in reverse chronological order, and for popular authors it runs to thousands of entries, so pagination appears once more, this time via a property-specific rvcontinue token that the generic continue block carries for us. We request the revision ID, timestamp, editor name, and article size at each revision, but not the article content itself, which keeps the responses small.

rows = []
for page_id in authors["page_id"]:
    params = {
        "action": "query",
        "format": "json",
        "prop": "revisions",
        "rvprop": "ids|timestamp|user|size",
        "rvlimit": "max",
        "pageids": page_id
    }
    while True:
        data = polite_get(base_url, params).json()
        page = data["query"]["pages"][str(page_id)]
        for rev in page.get("revisions", []):
            rows.append({
                "page_id": page_id,
                "rev_id": rev["revid"],
                "timestamp": rev["timestamp"],
                "user": rev.get("user"),
                "size": rev["size"]
            })
        if "continue" not in data:
            break
        params.update(data["continue"])

revisions = (
    pl.DataFrame(rows)
    .with_columns(
        timestamp = c.timestamp.str.to_datetime("%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ")
    )
)
revisions

This is the largest table in the corpus by far and the most expensive to collect, which makes it the clearest illustration of why the cache is the raw layer: nobody wants to repeat this download because a downstream parsing decision changed. Note the rev_id column. Each value permanently identifies one exact version of one page, and the Action API can return the content of any revision on request. If an analysis ever needs to be pinned to the precise article text we saw today, these IDs are how.

title: 12  Data APIs and Corpora
text:
12.12 Page View Statistics
How often people read each article comes from a different service: the Wikimedia REST API, which serves analytics data. REST APIs encode the request in the structure of the URL path rather than in a parameter dictionary, so this section doubles as an introduction to the other major API style you will encounter. The endpoint for per-article page views looks like this:
https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/metrics/pageviews/per-article/
    {project}/{access}/{agent}/{article}/{granularity}/{start}/{end}
Each path segment is a choice. We use the en.wikipedia project, all-access to combine desktop and mobile, the user agent class to count human readers and exclude bots, daily granularity, and a fixed date range covering the 2025 calendar year. Fixing the range explicitly, rather than asking for “the last N days”, means a rerun of this code requests the same data rather than a window that drifts with the current date. The article title goes into the path with spaces replaced by underscores and any special characters percent-encoded, which is what quote handles.
Notice rest_v1 sitting in the URL. This is API versioning: the provider promises that endpoints under v1 keep behaving the same way, and breaking changes arrive under a new version prefix instead. Version numbers in URLs are one of the quiet foundations of reproducible pipelines, since code that pins a version does not break when the provider moves on.
One wrinkle is that a small number of pages have no view data at all, and the API reports this with a 404 status rather than an empty list. Our polite_get helper would raise an error on a 404, so this loop calls the session directly and treats the status code as information: 404 means skip, anything else non-200 is a real problem.

pv_base = (
    "https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/metrics/pageviews/per-article/"
    "en.wikipedia/all-access/user/{article}/daily/20250101/20251231"
)

rows = []
for title in authors["title"]:
    article = quote(title.replace(" ", "_"), safe="")
    session.cookies.clear()
    response = session.get(pv_base.format(article=article), headers=headers)
    if not getattr(response, "from_cache", False):
        time.sleep(0.2)
    if response.status_code == 404:
        continue
    response.raise_for_status()
    for item in response.json()["items"]:
        rows.append({
            "article": item["article"],
            "date": item["timestamp"],
            "views": item["views"]
        })

views = (
    pl.DataFrame(rows)
    .with_columns(date = c.date.str.slice(0, 8).str.to_date("%Y%m%d"))
    .join(
        authors.select(
            c.page_id,
            article = c.title.str.replace_all(" ", "_")
        ),
        on=c.article
    )
    .select(c.page_id, c.date, c.views)
)
views

The timestamps arrive as strings such as 2025010100, so we slice off the trailing hour digits and parse the rest as a date, then join the underscore-form titles back to the authors table to recover the page ID. As a check that the collection worked, here are the daily views for the three Brontë sisters across the year.

brontes = (
    views
    .join(authors.select(c.page_id, c.title), on=c.page_id)
    .filter(c.title.str.contains("Brontë"))
)

(
    ggplot(brontes, aes(c.date, c.views, color=c.title))
    .geom_line()
)








The series show weekly rhythms and occasional sharp spikes, and the sisters do not move together. Explaining patterns like these is the business of the temporal data chapter; here we only needed to see that the data came through intact.

title: 12  Data APIs and Corpora
text:
12.13 Language Links
English Wikipedia is one wiki among hundreds, and most of our authors have articles in other languages too. The langlinks property lists, for each page, the languages that have a corresponding article and its title there. Unlike text extracts, this property batches: we can send fifty page IDs per request, using the same chunking trick throughout, with continue pagination inside each batch in case the language lists overflow a response.

ids = authors["page_id"].to_list()
batches = [ids[i:(i + 50)] for i in range(0, len(ids), 50)]

rows = []
for batch in batches:
    params = {
        "action": "query",
        "format": "json",
        "prop": "langlinks",
        "lllimit": "max",
        "pageids": "|".join(str(i) for i in batch)
    }
    while True:
        data = polite_get(base_url, params).json()
        for page in data["query"]["pages"].values():
            for link in page.get("langlinks", []):
                rows.append({
                    "page_id": page["pageid"],
                    "lang": link["lang"],
                    "title": link["*"]
                })
        if "continue" not in data:
            break
        params.update(data["continue"])

langlinks = pl.DataFrame(rows)
langlinks
